EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted and actually worsened during September and October.  October, climatologically the driest month of the year anyway, fell even short of normal values at most locations in north and central Georgia.  Rainfall deficits of 15 to 20 inches were common in the north and rainfall deficits of 6 to 12 inches were common in central areas.     Most areas of the state had only received 30 to 40 percent of normal annual rainfall by the end of October.  Many lakes and rivers across north and central Georgia were nearing all time record low levels and above ground water supplies were being significantly impacted in many of the larger cities, especially Atlanta.  A number of stream gage locations on creeks and river in north and central Georgia had established new record low water levels during October, including the major river basins of the Coosa, Chattahoochee, upper Oconee, upper Ocmulgee and Flint. Significant water conservation measures were being implemented in many cities across north and central Georgia.||Note, the impact of the drought on crops and the estimated monetary damage was included with the September 2007 Storm Data.  Additional crop losses from September to October should be minimal in comparison as October marks the end of the growing season.

A very deep closed upper low was cutoff over the mid-south.  An occluded front moved out ahead of the upper low and through north and central Georgia during the afternoon hours of the 23rd.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along this front in an unseasonably warm, moist atmosphere present over the region.  Thunderstorms did not reach severe limits, but a church pavillion under construction was damaged by thunderstorm winds in Bartow county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Tribune News of Cartersville reported that a thunderstorm wind gust caused a pavillion, under construction, to be blown down at the Trinity United Methodist Church on West Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted and actually worsened during September and October.  October, climatologically the driest month of the year anyway, fell even short of normal values at most locations in north and central Georgia.  Rainfall deficits of 15 to 20 inches were common in the north and rainfall deficits of 6 to 12 inches were common in central areas.     Most areas of the state had only received 30 to 40 percent of normal annual rainfall by the end of October.  Many lakes and rivers across north and central Georgia were nearing all time record low levels and above ground water supplies were being significantly impacted in many of the larger cities, especially Atlanta.  A number of stream gage locations on creeks and river in north and central Georgia had established new record low water levels during October, including the major river basins of the Coosa, Chattahoochee, upper Oconee, upper Ocmulgee and Flint. Significant water conservation measures were being implemented in many cities across north and central Georgia.||Note, the impact of the drought on crops and the estimated monetary damage was included with the September 2007 Storm Data.  Additional crop losses from September to October should be minimal in comparison as October marks the end of the growing season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire broke out April 27th and continued into May. Roundabout On May 3rd the fire was 5,857 acres and 50 percent contained. This fire started on private land four miles west of Pearson and burned southern rough and timber as fuels. Residences and a sewage treatment center were threatened early in the month. By the 10th the fire had reached 95 percent containment with 225 personnel working the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fire broke out in the Kneeknocker Swamp near Nahunta, Brantley County, Georgia on or about April 16th and continued into May. At its height the fire threatened 150 homes, 75 businesses and other critical infrastructure and forced the evaction of 400 individuals. More than 100 firefighters fought the blaze with several injuried during a breakout on April 26th around 4 p.m. EDT. By the end of the month the fire had consumed 1,321 acres and was 55 percent contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire broke out in the Kneeknocker Swamp near Nahunta, Brantley County, Georgia on or about April 16th and continued into May. At its height the fire threatened 150 homes, 75 businesses and other critical infrastructure and forced the evaction of 400 individuals. More than 100 firefighters fought the blaze with several injuried during a breakout on April 26th around 4 p.m. EDT. By the end of the month the fire had consumed 1,321 acres and was 55 percent contained.

Many lakes and rivers across north and central Georgia were nearing all time record low levels and above ground water supplies were being significantly impacted in many of the larger cities, especially Atlanta.  A number of stream gage locations on creeks and river in north and central Georgia had established new record low water levels during October, including the major river basins of the Coosa, Chattahoochee, upper Oconee, upper Ocmulgee and Flint. Significant water conservation measures were being implemented in many cities across north and central Georgia.||Note, the impact of the drought on crops and the estimated monetary damage was included with the September 2007 Storm Data.  Additional crop losses from September to October should be minimal in comparison as October marks the end of the growing season.

Light to occasionally moderate snow developed across the mountainous areas away from the Tennessee border during the early evening and continued through the overnight hours. Snowfall totals ranged from 2-3 inches.

An area of low pressure developed near the central Gulf coast states and tracked northeast along a nearly stationary front across south Georgia during the late afternoon of December 30th.  Pre-frontal storms developed across south Georgia where surface based lifted indices were near -2 to -3. Storms had persistent mesocyclone rotations and several storms developed bow echo signatures with outbound velocities around 40 to 50 knots along the leading edge of the bow around 3-4 kft above ground level prior to warning issuance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county dispatch reported several trees were blown down along Quincey Batten Loop near State Road 206. The cost of tree removal was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with strong dynamics pushed into the area Saturday night and took down trees and powerlines and produced nickel size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway dept. reported trees down on Corley road east of Keysville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN spotters and public reported penny to nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bryan County Warning Point reported that several roads were still closed and under water due to heavy rainfall across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bryan County Warning Point reported numerous road closures across the county...especially in and around Richmond Hill. Several cars have been stalled and stranded due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was reported that Miner Drive was closed and blocked off in the Mulberry subdivision. Numerous streets were also reported to be closed on Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail on the west side of Trenton.  The Dade County Emergency Management Director observed pea-sized hail near Rising Fawn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual July cold front supported by a strong upper trough in the mid-Atlantic brought scattered thunderstorms to north and central Georgia during the afternoon. One of these briefly became severe in Cherokee county in north central Georgia producing quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter-sized hail in the area between Canton and southwest of Free Home near the Fulton county line, while the Cherokee County Emergency Management Director observed penny-sized hail on the east side of the county.  In addition, a couple of trees were down off Union Hill Road south of Free Home near the Fulton County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, and unstable lower atmosphere led to scattered afternoon and evening shower and thunderstorm development.  The strongest storms occurred in central sections of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that thunderstorm wind gusts blew a roof and several tin walls off a storage shed at Belda Farms incorporated in far easterly Dooly county near the Pulaski county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, and unstable lower atmosphere led to scattered afternoon and evening shower and thunderstorm development.  The strongest storms occurred in central sections of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, and unstable lower atmosphere led to scattered afternoon and evening shower and thunderstorm development.  The strongest storms occurred in central sections of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pulaski County 911 Center reported that several trees were down on U.S. Highway 341 south and Lower River Road, southeast of Hawkinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, and unstable lower atmosphere led to scattered afternoon and evening shower and thunderstorm development.  The strongest storms occurred in central sections of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilcox County Emergency Management Director and the Wilcox County 911 Center reported that several trees and power lines were down in the Pineview, Abbeville, and Rochelle areas.  Much of the Pineview area was left without power.  In addition, two delivery vehicles were blown off the road south of Rochelle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong late July cold front and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable atmosphere to produce one of the most widespread outbreaks of severe weather seen across the Peachtree City forecast area since mid-April.  The was marked by a mesoscale convective complex which began early in the day across northeast Alabama and quickly spread southeast through west central and into central Georgia during the afternoon.  Numerous reports of large hail were received and wind damage was widespread in the path of the convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to nickel-sized hail in Peachtree City.  Pea-sized hail was observed at the National Weather Service Forecast Office about two miles south of Peachtree City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Humberto was the only hurricane to strike the U.S. coastline during the 2007 hurricane season.  The hurricane, which rapidly strenghted from a tropical storm off the upper Texas coast late on the 12th, moved inland near the Texas-Louisiana border early on the 13th.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto then tracked across central Louisiana, central Mississippi, and into northern Alabama on the 14th.  The center of the remnant of the track moved across extreme northwest Georgia, Dade and Walker county, on the 14th.  With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reports that an apparent thunderstorm downburst or microburst resulted in six or more large trees down in a heavily wooded neighborhood in north Cobb county.  Streets included were Devonshire Drive, Buckingham Circle, and Sunset Circle.  One tree was down on a home, causing damage to the structure.  A couple of trees were down on power lines, and Devonshire Road was blocked from a downed tree.  Some of the trees were at least 100-years old.  In addition, a street sign was down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockdale Citizen of Conyers reported that lightning struck a home on Georgia Highway 20 entering the house via metallic water and gas lines.  A portion of the floor of the home was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gainesville Times reported that lightning struck several trees on McEver Road, toppling them and causing them to catch on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large upper low over the northeastern U.S. continued and was actually sinking a little further south toward the mid-Atlantic.  As a result, scattered thunderstorms developed once again during the heat of the afternoon from northerly flow and cold air aloft.  A few became severe producing marginal severe hail and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail on the north side of Peachtree City in the Kedron area.  One-quarter to one-half inch diameter hail was observed at the National Weather Service Forecast Office on the south side of Peachtree City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another short wave in northwest flow brought thunderstorms to mainly the northwest part of the state.  All but one remained below severe limits, however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter-sized hail at a marina on Lake Allatoona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 25th proved to be one of the more significant severe weather days during the month.  This was the result of a strong short wave in westerly flow aloft and a very unstable and moist atmosphere present over the area.  Damaging downburst winds, large hail, and most significantly a number of lightning incidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee of the Jackson Electric Membership Cooperative, which provides power to this local area, reported that six trees were down along Roy Parks Road east to northeast of Candler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 25th proved to be one of the more significant severe weather days during the month.  This was the result of a strong short wave in westerly flow aloft and a very unstable and moist atmosphere present over the area.  Damaging downburst winds, large hail, and most significantly a number of lightning incidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hall County 911 Center reported that lightning struck a building in the city causing minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Humberto was the only hurricane to strike the U.S. coastline during the 2007 hurricane season.  The hurricane, which rapidly strenghted from a tropical storm off the upper Texas coast late on the 12th, moved inland near the Texas-Louisiana border early on the 13th.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto then tracked across central Louisiana, central Mississippi, and into northern Alabama on the 14th.  The center of the remnant of the track moved across extreme northwest Georgia, Dade and Walker county, on the 14th.  With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, and unstable lower atmosphere led to scattered afternoon and evening shower and thunderstorm development.  The strongest storms occurred in central sections of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta-Journal & Constitution reported that a 29-year old male was struck by lightning while doing construction work on a building in the Sandy Springs area.  Co-workers used a forklift to move him to the ground where he was treated by emergency medical technicians.  He was treated on site and then transported to a local hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Newton Citizen of Rockdale reported that lightning struck a home on Williams Road near Georgia Highway 81.  Extensive damage resulted to the attic.  The four occupants were displaced from their home.  Lightning also knocked out the power line to a home on Georgia Highway 142, but there was no fire reported that this site.

With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was located across central Georgia.  Meanwhile...rich tropical moisture was flowing into the region from the Gulf as Hurricane Humberto moved near the upper Texas coast.  This resulted in the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across central and north Georgia during the afternoon.  While none of these reached severe limits, they did produce frequent cloud-to-ground lightning and locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Department of Transportation reported that lightning struck a transformer on a power pole adjacent to interstate-85 near Fairburn.  This resulted in the attached power line falling down on the interstate.  All lanes of the interstate had to be closed while the line was repaired.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a fall cold front moving into the area from the northwest.  An upper-level trough was also moving through the area from the northwest.  These systems supported the development of scattered afternoon thunderstorms across much of north and central Georgia. A few became strong and at least one became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fall cold front was moving through the area from the northwest aided by a vigorous upper-level trough moving through the Ohio Valley into the mid-Atlantic region.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along the cold front during the day and spread southeastward during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post reported that lightning struck the attic of a home on Tribble Gates Court within the Gwinnett county portion of Loganville.  Damage was minor to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and cold front were approaching the area from the west late in the day.  A band of light to moderate rain preceded these weather systems across west Georgia late in the day.  The leading edge of this rain area brought strong winds to parts of northwest Georgia, causing some trees and power lines to be blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County 911 Center reported that numerous trees and power lines were down throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong late July cold front and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable atmosphere to produce one of the most widespread outbreaks of severe weather seen across the Peachtree City forecast area since mid-April.  The was marked by a mesoscale convective complex which began early in the day across northeast Alabama and quickly spread southeast through west central and into central Georgia during the afternoon.  Numerous reports of large hail were received and wind damage was widespread in the path of the convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Times Herald of Newnan reported that lightning struck a tree approximately 50 feet from a home on Tabby Lynch Road.  The lightning travelled underground on a gas line and ignited the home on fire.  The home was declared a total loss.  It took a crew of 12 firefighters 1 1/2 hours to put out the fire.  No one was home at the time of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 25th proved to be one of the more significant severe weather days during the month.  This was the result of a strong short wave in westerly flow aloft and a very unstable and moist atmosphere present over the area.  Damaging downburst winds, large hail, and most significantly a number of lightning incidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County 911 Center reported that a power line was down about one mile west of Americus on U.S. Highway 280.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same upper low that brought a number of large hail storms to north and central Georgia on June 11th and 12th remained over the northeast, but was shifting slightly further east.  Isolated thunderstorms, a few severe, continued to develop during the afternoon, but were confined primarily to north Georgia on this day.  Several produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning, but only isolated storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail.

A few became severe producing marginal severe hail and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that seven to eight trees were down in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An un-seasonal amplified upper level trough and attendant surface front moved into the forecast area during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong differential positive vorticity advection, high surface based instability, large CAPE in the hail growth zone and low level forcing spawned widespread severe storms. Aloft cool 500 mb around -10 degrees Celsius were noted and mid level dry air moved over the area, and both factors increased downdraft strength in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported penny size hail in an isolated area of Highway 177, about 8 miles Northeast of Fargo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Humberto was the only hurricane to strike the U.S. coastline during the 2007 hurricane season.  The hurricane, which rapidly strenghted from a tropical storm off the upper Texas coast late on the 12th, moved inland near the Texas-Louisiana border early on the 13th.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto then tracked across central Louisiana, central Mississippi, and into northern Alabama on the 14th.  The center of the remnant of the track moved across extreme northwest Georgia, Dade and Walker county, on the 14th.  With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began during the pre-dawn hours as light snow across the piedmont of extreme northeast Georgia. The precipitation became heavy at times around sunrise, and began to mix with sleet and freezing rain. By mid-morning, as much as 2 inches of snow had fallen, with light accumulations of sleet reported in some areas. A mix of sleet and freezing rain continued through much of the morning. By mid-morning, up to a quarter inch of ice and as much as a half inch of sleet had accumulated on top of 1-3 inches of snow. By late morning, most of the precipitation had transitioned to rain.

A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to quarter-sized hail from Dawsonville to near the intersection of Georgia Highway 400 and Georgia Highway 53.  The Dawson County 911 Center reported that nickel-sized hail fell in downtown Dawsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public and the Dawson County 911 Center reported that many trees were down throughout Dawsonville and south and east of Dawsonville.  The roof was blown off a store in the southeast part of Dawsonville and onto an adjacent vehicle.  The public estimated that winds were in the 50 to 60 mph range for up to 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, and unstable lower atmosphere led to scattered afternoon and evening shower and thunderstorm development.  The strongest storms occurred in central sections of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Sheriff reported that two trees were blown down on Georgia Highway 300 near mile marker five west of Wenona.  He estimated winds at 60 mph.  The Crisp County 911 Center reported that an additional two trees were down on Old Albany Road near Raines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County 911 Center reported that at least six trees were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong late July cold front and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable atmosphere to produce one of the most widespread outbreaks of severe weather seen across the Peachtree City forecast area since mid-April.  The was marked by a mesoscale convective complex which began early in the day across northeast Alabama and quickly spread southeast through west central and into central Georgia during the afternoon.  Numerous reports of large hail were received and wind damage was widespread in the path of the convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pike County 911 Center reported that several trees and power lines were down in the southeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as snow during the early morning hours across the mountains and foothills of Georgia. The snow became moderate to heavy at times by sunrise. As much as 3 inches of snow had accumulated by 6 am. Snow, heavy at times continued during the mid and late morning hours across the mountains and foothills, with heavy snowfall totals occurring in most locations. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain over the foothills. Total snowfall accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were reported across the area by the time the snow tapered off around noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 25th proved to be one of the more significant severe weather days during the month.  This was the result of a strong short wave in westerly flow aloft and a very unstable and moist atmosphere present over the area.  Damaging downburst winds, large hail, and most significantly a number of lightning incidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSB Television of Atlanta reported that a 27-year old male construction worker was killed when struck by lightning.  The individual was working on a house under construction.  He and other workers sought shelter in the structure during the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While high pressure aloft was firmly anchored over the southeastern U.S., weak short waves in northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, unstable air mass allowed for strong thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon once again.  One of these briefly became severe in Fannin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge over the southeast U.S. weakened some as a strong upper trough was moving through the Ohio Valley and northeast U.S.  A weak nearly stationary front was located across the Tennessee Valley.  These systems were close enough to combine with moisture and afternoon heating and instability to produce scattered thunderstorms once again, a few of which became severe and produced marginal severe hail and downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny to nickel-sized hail in extreme eastern Putnam county near the Hancock county line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows broken out of a restaurant and signs blown down at a service station. Also, several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Metal roof blown off of a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over northeast Georgia for a second day in a row.
Scattered severe storms developed over northeast Georgia for a second day in a row.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chatham County 911 dispatch center reported approximately 15 streets or intersections blocked due to standing water. Law  enforcement was blockading many of these areas as quickly as possible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported that numerous roads were closed due to high water in the Richmond Hill area of Bryan County.  In addition, water was also reported to be entering several homes in subdivisions around the Richmond Hill area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed nickel-sized hail north of Ringgold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, and unstable lower atmosphere led to scattered afternoon and evening shower and thunderstorm development.  The strongest storms occurred in central sections of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockdale Citizen of Conyers reported that many trees were blown down throughout much of the county from strong straight-line winds.  The majority of the downed trees were in the north and east part of the county.  Trees were down on Bethel Road, Old Covington Road, Susong Road, and Dennard Road.  However, no power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong late July cold front and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable atmosphere to produce one of the most widespread outbreaks of severe weather seen across the Peachtree City forecast area since mid-April.  The was marked by a mesoscale convective complex which began early in the day across northeast Alabama and quickly spread southeast through west central and into central Georgia during the afternoon.  Numerous reports of large hail were received and wind damage was widespread in the path of the convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montgomery County 911 Center reported that four trees were down within the county, while the public reported that an additional tree was down within the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary extended through southern South Carolina and Southeast Georgia, and when combined with numerous boundaries and rich tropical airmass, resulted in a couple Flash Flood and Severe Weather events across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees and numerous tree limbs were reported down due to high winds. The damage occurred on Georgia Avenue and Chelsea Circle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported on Delano Street, Winburn Street, Hastings  Street, Cornwall Street, and at the intersection of Gwynnett and  Stiles Streets.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hall County 911 Center reported that 10 trees were down across the county, mainly the western and southern portions.  Pea-sized hail was also reported in Gainesville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County Emergency Management Director reported that there was widespread wind damage across Carroll county as a result of thunderstorm wind gusts.  Many trees and a number of power lines were down across the county from Mt. Zion to Clem.  The most concentrated damage was in the Carrollton area.  Three homes sustained moderate damage from downed trees.  Eight other homes suffered minor damage as a result of downed trees.  A truck was totaled when a large tree fell on it.  At least 3800 people in the county were left without power during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual summer cold front continued to move southeast through Georgia.  By afternoon it had moved through the northwest corner of the state.  Northwest flow and cold air aloft continued to provide an unstable air mass across the area.  This combined with daytime heating led to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms once again.  Isolated storms became severe and most produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fire & Rescue Department for Montgomery county reported that four trees were down on power lines on the east side of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a Kroger food store.  Smoke was reported in the building, but damage was determined to be minor by the local fire department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree, some large limbs, and power lines were blown down between Cornelia and Mt Airy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the late afternoon and evening hours.

Precipitation began as snow during the early morning hours across the mountains and foothills of Georgia. The snow became moderate to heavy at times by sunrise. As much as 3 inches of snow had accumulated by 6 am. Snow, heavy at times continued during the mid and late morning hours across the mountains and foothills, with heavy snowfall totals occurring in most locations. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain over the foothills. Total snowfall accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were reported across the area by the time the snow tapered off around noon.

A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail in the Locust Grove area and penny-sized hail in the Hampton and Luella areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge over the southeast U.S. weakened some as a strong upper trough was moving through the Ohio Valley and northeast U.S.  A weak nearly stationary front was located across the Tennessee Valley.  These systems were close enough to combine with moisture and afternoon heating and instability to produce scattered thunderstorms once again, a few of which became severe and produced marginal severe hail and downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Warren County Emergency Management Director reported that an apparent thunderstorm downburst from a small thunderstorm resulted in six to nine trees being blown down in the area south and east of Warrenten.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge over the southeast U.S. weakened some as a strong upper trough was moving through the Ohio Valley and northeast U.S.  A weak nearly stationary front was located across the Tennessee Valley.  These systems were close enough to combine with moisture and afternoon heating and instability to produce scattered thunderstorms once again, a few of which became severe and produced marginal severe hail and downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Warren County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a home.  The home was completely destroyed from the resulting fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While high pressure aloft was firmly anchored over the southeastern U.S., weak short waves in northwest flow aloft and a very warm, moist, unstable air mass allowed for strong thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon once again.  One of these briefly became severe in Fannin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported that three trees were blown down in the Friendship area northwest of Cumming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced wind damage just north of the Seminole-Miller County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two small farm sheds lost their roofs.  One irrigation system was overturned along with three uprooted pecan trees.  The damage occurred just north of the Seminole-Miller County line near Grady Cobb Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted and actually worsened during September and October.  October, climatologically the driest month of the year anyway, fell even short of normal values at most locations in north and central Georgia.  Rainfall deficits of 15 to 20 inches were common in the north and rainfall deficits of 6 to 12 inches were common in central areas.     Most areas of the state had only received 30 to 40 percent of normal annual rainfall by the end of October.  Many lakes and rivers across north and central Georgia were nearing all time record low levels and above ground water supplies were being significantly impacted in many of the larger cities, especially Atlanta.  A number of stream gage locations on creeks and river in north and central Georgia had established new record low water levels during October, including the major river basins of the Coosa, Chattahoochee, upper Oconee, upper Ocmulgee and Flint. Significant water conservation measures were being implemented in many cities across north and central Georgia.||Note, the impact of the drought on crops and the estimated monetary damage was included with the September 2007 Storm Data.  Additional crop losses from September to October should be minimal in comparison as October marks the end of the growing season.

A healthy fall cold front was moving through north Georgia during the afternoon hours.  This combined with ample low-level moisture allowed scattered thunderstorms to develop.  None reached severe limits, but one in Gwinnett county did causing some lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a shed behind a home on Tina Court.  The storage shed was set on fire, causing damage to the structure and lawn equipment stored within.

The latter half of December saw a transition to a wetter pattern across the southeast, which much of extreme northeast Georgia receiving above normal monthly rainfall for the first time since January 2007. However, this was not enough to put much of a dent in the long-term drought as extreme to exceptional drought conditions persisted into the New Year. Although the increase in rainfall did allow for some recharge of area streams, most were still running at less than 25 percent of normal flow at the end of the month. Lake Hartwell recorded the 3rd lowest December average pool level on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty northwest winds developed behind a dry wintertime cold front that moved through the area on the 5th.  This helped fuel a wildfire in northwest Georgia during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rome News Tribune reported that a wildfire was sparked by a truck traveling along Georgia Highway 101 near Zona Kay Road in the far southern portion of the county near the Polk county line.  The quickly spread through the dormant vegetation on the heels on 15 to 25 mph gusty, northwest winds.  The fire was extinguished before any structures were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a severe storm that produced 1.75 inch hail near Willacoochee. Twelve minutes later law enforcement reported the hail had decreased in size to 0.75 inch near Pearson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Barry made landfall along the Florida west coast during the afternoon of June 2nd. The storm then advanced north-northeast along the Atlantic coast through June 3rd. The main impacts of the storm on our area were heavy rains and gusty winds, which downed trees. Storm total rainfall amounts neared 6 inches across portions of the forecast area. Wind advisories were issued for coastal zones and lake wind advisories for the remainder of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 845 pm local time, a spotter reported numerous trees down along U.S. Highway 441 from Homerville northward to near the Atkinson county line. There were also widespread power outages. Around 9 pm local time, the spotter reported that a large tree had fallen on a trailer home caving in the roof in Du Pont. The man inside the trailer was not hurt.

Hurricane Humberto was the only hurricane to strike the U.S. coastline during the 2007 hurricane season.  The hurricane, which rapidly strenghted from a tropical storm off the upper Texas coast late on the 12th, moved inland near the Texas-Louisiana border early on the 13th.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto then tracked across central Louisiana, central Mississippi, and into northern Alabama on the 14th.  The center of the remnant of the track moved across extreme northwest Georgia, Dade and Walker county, on the 14th.  With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November provided no relief from the effects of the long term drought. In fact, another month of well-below normal rainfall made an already dire situation even worse. Many locations remained on pace to set annual records for rainfall deficit. By the end of the month, the vast majority of the region was experiencing exceptional drought conditions. Streamflow on area rivers remained extremely low,  generally less than 10 percent of normal. Meanwhile, lakes continued to gradually fall toward record low levels, including Lake Hartwell, which was only 3 to 4 feet above the record by the end of November.

A short wave upper level trough passed over the area during the afternoon and evening and enhanced sea breeze induced convection across the area. High instability and moisture values caused wet microbursts, including in Flagler county where 58-60 mph wind gusts were measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A side road on the westside of Homerville was covered by 4 to 6 inches of water. Two to four inches of standing water also covered portions of Highway 84 just east of Homerville.

With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November provided no relief from the effects of the long term drought. In fact, another month of well-below normal rainfall made an already dire situation even worse. Many locations remained on pace to set annual records for rainfall deficit. By the end of the month, the vast majority of the region was experiencing exceptional drought conditions. Streamflow on area rivers remained extremely low,  generally less than 10 percent of normal. Meanwhile, lakes continued to gradually fall toward record low levels, including Lake Hartwell, which was only 3 to 4 feet above the record by the end of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave upper level trough passed over the area during the afternoon and evening and enhanced sea breeze induced convection across the area. High instability and moisture values caused wet microbursts, including in Flagler county where 58-60 mph wind gusts were measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tress were blown down in an RV park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave upper level trough passed over the area during the afternoon and evening and enhanced sea breeze induced convection across the area. High instability and moisture values caused wet microbursts, including in Flagler county where 58-60 mph wind gusts were measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in Screven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Humberto was the only hurricane to strike the U.S. coastline during the 2007 hurricane season.  The hurricane, which rapidly strenghted from a tropical storm off the upper Texas coast late on the 12th, moved inland near the Texas-Louisiana border early on the 13th.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto then tracked across central Louisiana, central Mississippi, and into northern Alabama on the 14th.  The center of the remnant of the track moved across extreme northwest Georgia, Dade and Walker county, on the 14th.  With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the entire state during May.  Rainfall deficits across many counties of north and central Georgia continued to grow as well as the number of counties classified in severe and extreme drought conditions.  By the end of May 2007, 74 Georgia counties were classified as being in extreme drought, 79 in severe drought, and six in moderate drought.  Counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area classified as being in extreme drought include Bartow, Carroll, Catoosa, Chattooga, Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, Coweta, Dade, DeKalb, Douglas, Fannin, Fayette, Floyd, Fulton, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Harris, Heard, Meriwether, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Towns, Troup, Union, Walker and Whitfield.  Drought conditions in all remaining counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were classified as severe.  Rainfall deficits as of May 31, 2007 for some of the major north and central Georgia cities were:||Athens:  6:49 inches|Columbus:  8.68 inches|Macon:  9.30 inches|Atlanta:  10.02 inches|Plains:  10.09 inches|Blairsville:  13.89 inches|LaFayette:  14.06 inches||Macon observed its driest May in history with only a trace of precipitation.||Record to near-record low stream flows were reported by the end of May across the state, including the Coosawattee River near Ellijay, the Oostanaula at Resaca and Rome, the Middle Oconee near Athens, Oconee at Milledgeville and Dublin, and the Ocmulgee near Jackson and at Lumber City.  Groundwater levels were dropping statewide.  Many wells are approaching their average yearly low water level, normally reached in late summer or early fall.   Much of the state remained under level-2 outdoor water restrictions, with total bans on outdoor watering in some west central Georgia counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the entire state during May.  Rainfall deficits across many counties of north and central Georgia continued to grow as well as the number of counties classified in severe and extreme drought conditions.  By the end of May 2007, 74 Georgia counties were classified as being in extreme drought, 79 in severe drought, and six in moderate drought.  Counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area classified as being in extreme drought include Bartow, Carroll, Catoosa, Chattooga, Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, Coweta, Dade, DeKalb, Douglas, Fannin, Fayette, Floyd, Fulton, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Harris, Heard, Meriwether, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Towns, Troup, Union, Walker and Whitfield.  Drought conditions in all remaining counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were classified as severe.  Rainfall deficits as of May 31, 2007 for some of the major north and central Georgia cities were:||Athens:  6:49 inches|Columbus:  8.68 inches|Macon:  9.30 inches|Atlanta:  10.02 inches|Plains:  10.09 inches|Blairsville:  13.89 inches|LaFayette:  14.06 inches||Macon observed its driest May in history with only a trace of precipitation.||Record to near-record low stream flows were reported by the end of May across the state, including the Coosawattee River near Ellijay, the Oostanaula at Resaca and Rome, the Middle Oconee near Athens, Oconee at Milledgeville and Dublin, and the Ocmulgee near Jackson and at Lumber City.  Groundwater levels were dropping statewide.  Many wells are approaching their average yearly low water level, normally reached in late summer or early fall.   Much of the state remained under level-2 outdoor water restrictions, with total bans on outdoor watering in some west central Georgia counties.

An isolated severe storm produced large hail in extreme northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping-pong ball size hail reported on Seed Lake Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small trees were blown down near the south end of Lake Burton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 3 to 4 inch diameter tree limbs blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Whispering Pines Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level TUTT moved across south Florida as the sea breezes and a weak surface front sagged into south Georgia. Dynamics around the upper level trough combined with high moisture and instability in addition to low level forcing from the front and sea breezes spawned isolated severe storms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail was reported about 5 miles southwest of Homerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level TUTT moved across south Florida as the sea breezes and a weak surface front sagged into south Georgia. Dynamics around the upper level trough combined with high moisture and instability in addition to low level forcing from the front and sea breezes spawned isolated severe storms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down across the road on the east side of Dupont. There were also 2 brush fires that started in the area from frequent lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front across South Georgia and the sea breezes spawned afternoon and evening thunderstorms in a moderately unstable and moist airmass. Some upper level positive vorticity advection rotating over the area around the northwest side of a retrograding upper level ridge across Florida enhanced convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Doppler radar indicated near 2 inches of rain fell west of Argyle and east of Homerville along U.S. Highway 84 in the past hour. A trained spotter reported localized flooding and up to 6 inches of standing water on U.S. Highway 84.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front across South Georgia and the sea breezes spawned afternoon and evening thunderstorms in a moderately unstable and moist airmass. Some upper level positive vorticity advection rotating over the area around the northwest side of a retrograding upper level ridge across Florida enhanced convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down in Camden county.  The most damaged was reported near Kingsland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced wet microbursts with several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were down across the eastern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced wet microbursts with several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were down.  One fell on a trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced wet microbursts with several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees fell on two houses along Adams Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced wet microbursts with several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were down.  One fell on a house along Parkwood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced wet microbursts with several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second storm downed several large trees across the eastern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced widespread wind damage in Tift County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and utility poles were down throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees blown down in and around Clayton, with some trees damaging homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A isolated severe storm affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three or 4 trees blown down on power lines in the Reed Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary associated with a convective complex over the Atlantic waters moved inland during the late afternoon. This boundary as well as the sea breeze fronts produced numerous severe storms across the forecast area in a high moisture and high instability environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down all over the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary associated with a convective complex over the Atlantic waters moved inland during the late afternoon. This boundary as well as the sea breeze fronts produced numerous severe storms across the forecast area in a high moisture and high instability environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 10-15 trees were blown down across various roads throughout the  county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from a large MCS that developed over Georgia pushed into our Georgia zones during the early morning hours.  In addition, an upper level short wave trough enhanced convection along the outflow. During the afternoon, the lingering outflow and sea breezes spawned more storms in a moist and unstable airmass over our Georgia zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one foot of standing water accumulated on U.S. Highway 84 in Du Pont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated microburst affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees blown down, with 1 tree on a house, and another on a mobile home. The mobile home received some damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary associated with a convective complex over the Atlantic waters moved inland during the late afternoon. This boundary as well as the sea breeze fronts produced numerous severe storms across the forecast area in a high moisture and high instability environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level TUTT moved across south Florida as the sea breezes and a weak surface front sagged into south Georgia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Old Douglas Highway, about 2 miles north of  Pearson. The downed trees contributed to a traffic accident along the  highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from a large MCS that developed over Georgia pushed into our Georgia zones during the early morning hours.  In addition, an upper level short wave trough enhanced convection along the outflow. During the afternoon, the lingering outflow and sea breezes spawned more storms in a moist and unstable airmass over our Georgia zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike damaged 5 parked vehicles. The event was caught on a security camera. Damaged cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the entire state during May.  Rainfall deficits across many counties of north and central Georgia continued to grow as well as the number of counties classified in severe and extreme drought conditions.  By the end of May 2007, 74 Georgia counties were classified as being in extreme drought, 79 in severe drought, and six in moderate drought.  Counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area classified as being in extreme drought include Bartow, Carroll, Catoosa, Chattooga, Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, Coweta, Dade, DeKalb, Douglas, Fannin, Fayette, Floyd, Fulton, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Harris, Heard, Meriwether, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Towns, Troup, Union, Walker and Whitfield.  Drought conditions in all remaining counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were classified as severe.  Rainfall deficits as of May 31, 2007 for some of the major north and central Georgia cities were:||Athens:  6:49 inches|Columbus:  8.68 inches|Macon:  9.30 inches|Atlanta:  10.02 inches|Plains:  10.09 inches|Blairsville:  13.89 inches|LaFayette:  14.06 inches||Macon observed its driest May in history with only a trace of precipitation.||Record to near-record low stream flows were reported by the end of May across the state, including the Coosawattee River near Ellijay, the Oostanaula at Resaca and Rome, the Middle Oconee near Athens, Oconee at Milledgeville and Dublin, and the Ocmulgee near Jackson and at Lumber City.  Groundwater levels were dropping statewide.  Many wells are approaching their average yearly low water level, normally reached in late summer or early fall.   Much of the state remained under level-2 outdoor water restrictions, with total bans on outdoor watering in some west central Georgia counties.

Weak surface low pressure over central Georgia in combination with sea breeze allowed for an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop.

Drought conditions continued to worsen across the entire state during May.  Rainfall deficits across many counties of north and central Georgia continued to grow as well as the number of counties classified in severe and extreme drought conditions.  By the end of May 2007, 74 Georgia counties were classified as being in extreme drought, 79 in severe drought, and six in moderate drought.  Counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area classified as being in extreme drought include Bartow, Carroll, Catoosa, Chattooga, Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, Coweta, Dade, DeKalb, Douglas, Fannin, Fayette, Floyd, Fulton, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Harris, Heard, Meriwether, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Towns, Troup, Union, Walker and Whitfield.  Drought conditions in all remaining counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were classified as severe.  Rainfall deficits as of May 31, 2007 for some of the major north and central Georgia cities were:||Athens:  6:49 inches|Columbus:  8.68 inches|Macon:  9.30 inches|Atlanta:  10.02 inches|Plains:  10.09 inches|Blairsville:  13.89 inches|LaFayette:  14.06 inches||Macon observed its driest May in history with only a trace of precipitation.||Record to near-record low stream flows were reported by the end of May across the state, including the Coosawattee River near Ellijay, the Oostanaula at Resaca and Rome, the Middle Oconee near Athens, Oconee at Milledgeville and Dublin, and the Ocmulgee near Jackson and at Lumber City. A late season cold snap produced widespread frost and freezing temperatures across much of Georgia.  Overall damage to the state was at least a 100 million dollar loss due to damaged fruit and vegetable plants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dipped down into the 20s most areas which produced widespread damage to crops and fruit trees.  Total monetary losses unknown but significant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the entire state during May.  Rainfall deficits across many counties of north and central Georgia continued to grow as well as the number of counties classified in severe and extreme drought conditions.  By the end of May 2007, 74 Georgia counties were classified as being in extreme drought, 79 in severe drought, and six in moderate drought.  Counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area classified as being in extreme drought include Bartow, Carroll, Catoosa, Chattooga, Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, Coweta, Dade, DeKalb, Douglas, Fannin, Fayette, Floyd, Fulton, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Harris, Heard, Meriwether, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Towns, Troup, Union, Walker and Whitfield.  Drought conditions in all remaining counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were classified as severe.  Rainfall deficits as of May 31, 2007 for some of the major north and central Georgia cities were:||Athens:  6:49 inches|Columbus:  8.68 inches|Macon:  9.30 inches|Atlanta:  10.02 inches|Plains:  10.09 inches|Blairsville:  13.89 inches|LaFayette:  14.06 inches||Macon observed its driest May in history with only a trace of precipitation.||Record to near-record low stream flows were reported by the end of May across the state, including the Coosawattee River near Ellijay, the Oostanaula at Resaca and Rome, the Middle Oconee near Athens, Oconee at Milledgeville and Dublin, and the Ocmulgee near Jackson and at Lumber City.  Groundwater levels were dropping statewide.  Many wells are approaching their average yearly low water level, normally reached in late summer or early fall.   Much of the state remained under level-2 outdoor water restrictions, with total bans on outdoor watering in some west central Georgia counties.

Unusually dry weather continued across northeast Georgia through October. Although a soaking rain near the end of the month resulted in near-normal monthly precipitation for the mountains, the Piedmont saw another month of well-below normal rainfall. Most areas were on pace to break yearly rainfall deficit records. By the end of the month, exceptional drought conditions were reported across the majority of the area. Water flow on area streams continued at 3 to 6 percent of normal, while lake levels remained at near-record lows. Although most cities and towns were requesting voluntary water restrictions be observed, mandatory restrictions were ordered in quite a few communities. Also, private wells were beginning to dry up in many areas. Agriculture continued to be severely impacted by the drought. At this time, county by county dollar estimates of drought damage have not been made available.

An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Meriwether County Emergency Management Director and 911 Center reported that several trees were blown down west of Greenville.  Some power lines were also down in the area.

The effects of an extended period of dry weather were exacerbated by an abnormally dry May, with many locations reporting one of the driest Mays in recorded history. By the end of May, many climatological stations were reporting yearly rainfall deficits as high as 10 inches. The result was severe to extreme drought conditions across northeast Georgia by the end of the month. The very dry conditions added to agriculture hardships caused by a hard freeze and widespread damaging winds in April.

The combination of high pressure building into the Southeast U.S. and low pressure off the South Carolina and Georgia coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across the area. Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce moderate to severe Beach Erosion across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant Beach Erosion took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that two homes were struck and set on fire by lightning.  One was on Wedgewood Chase Drive and the other on Williamsport Drive near East Jones Bridge Road, of which the roof was completely burned.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck and set on fire a home on Primrose Place.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Tribune News of Cartersville reported that lightning struck a home on Lazy Water Drive in Euharlee.  The attic was set on fire and about 75 to 100 percent of the attic was burned.  Other parts of the home also sustained smoke and water damage.  A double-wide mobile home was also struck by lightning in Taylorsville on Old Alabama Road.  This home was a total loss as a result of the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported golf ball-sized hail in the far southern portion of Bibb county near the Houston county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high pressure building into the Southeast U.S. and low pressure off the South Carolina and Georgia coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across the area. Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce moderate to severe Beach Erosion across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant Beach Erosion took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters.

The effects of an extended period of dry weather were exacerbated by an abnormally dry May, with many locations reporting one of the driest Mays in recorded history. By the end of May, many climatological stations were reporting yearly rainfall deficits as high as 10 inches. The result was severe to extreme drought conditions across northeast Georgia by the end of the month. The very dry conditions added to agriculture hardships caused by a hard freeze and widespread damaging winds in April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The effects of an extended period of dry weather were exacerbated by an abnormally dry May, with many locations reporting one of the driest Mays in recorded history. By the end of May, many climatological stations were reporting yearly rainfall deficits as high as 10 inches. The result was severe to extreme drought conditions across northeast Georgia by the end of the month. The very dry conditions added to agriculture hardships caused by a hard freeze and widespread damaging winds in April.
