EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service office in Peachtree City, GA confirmed that an F2 tornado had touched down approximately 8 miles southwest of Newnan near the intersection of Bohannon Road and New Corinth Road near Blalock Lake.  The tornado then traveled northeast between Old Corinth Road and New Corinth Road traveling approximately 4 miles to the northeast along Earl North Road before lifting.  As the tornado crossed Walker Brooks Road and Joe Brown Road just northeast of its touchdown point, a home was destroyed.  The winds in the tornado at this point were estimated to range between 125 and 135 mph.  As the tornado continued northeast along Earl North Road, several homes sustained minor to moderate damage.  A number of trees and at least two power lines were also downed along the path of the tornado.  Damage was also reported to a convenience store awning and several storage sheds were destroyed.  Portions of Earl North Road were blocked from downed trees and telephone poles.  While several residents, including a 14-year old boy were trapped in homes from debris and downed trees, no injuries were reported and all were rescued safely.  The tornado path length was approximately four miles long with a maximum path width of 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold snap resulted in record cold temperatures across the eastern half of the United States with record cold temperatures for the month of April and record late freezes reported locally.|Univ of Georgia Ag Agent.   Eighty percent of the blueberry crop was lost during the Easter weekend freeze.  11k acres are normally cultivated with blueberries with an average yield of 4m lb from the county.  Losses are likely to exceed $5M in this county alone. Pecans and blackberry also suffered damage but those losses are unknown at this time. |Ware and Clinch Counties...Indications are that Ware and Clinch Counties experienced an 80% crop loss with the Rabbiteye Blueberries (these are the later varieties.) The earlier Highbush Blueberries seemed to be almost undamaged due to overhead irrigation freeze protection measures.|Similar unconfirmed damage has been reported across most of southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record low temperature for the date and the month of April |was set at Alma, Georgia on the morning of April 8th. The low temperature of 30 degrees set the new record low temperature for both the date and month. The previous low temperature for this date was 34 in 1950 and for the month of April the previous record low temperature for the month was 31 in 1987.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitter Arctic air mass covered much of the eastern half of the nation on the 1st of February. However, because the upper-level trough was positively tilted into the southwest and south central U.S., the bulk of the cold air was spilling southward into the southern plains and eastward into the northeast U.S. A stationary front separating the cold Arctic air from warm, moist air, was located across south Georgia. Considerable lift was supplied over the shallow layer of cold air as a weak upper disturbance passed over the area early in the morning of the 1st. Temperatures were cold enough north of a Rome, to Canton, to Gainesville line for much of the precipitation to fall as snow. Some freezing rain and sleet was noted on the southern end of the frozen precipitation area, just grazing the northern suburbs of Atlanta. Snowfall amounts north of a Rome to Gainesville line were mostly around 2.0 inches, but some counties in the northeast Georgia mountains reported 5.0 - 6.0 inch snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walton County 911 Center reported less than 1/4 inch glaze ice on trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the southeast United States during the early morning hours of Sunday, February 25th.  While no severe weather was observed or reported within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, a couple of lines of thunderstorms, with occasional lightning and heavy rain, moved through the area during the hours of 2 am and 10 am.  Two lightning-related fires resulted in west central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Newnan Times-Herald reported that lightning struck an outbuilding on 140 Bill Hart Road.  The building housed a cabinet shop.

However, because the upper-level trough was positively tilted into the southwest and south central U.S., the bulk of the cold air was spilling southward into the southern plains and eastward into the northeast U.S. A stationary front separating the cold Arctic air from warm, moist air, was located across south Georgia. Considerable lift was supplied over the shallow layer of cold air as a weak upper disturbance passed over the area early in the morning of the 1st. Temperatures were cold enough north of a Rome, to Canton, to Gainesville line for much of the precipitation to fall as snow. Some freezing rain and sleet was noted on the southern end of the frozen precipitation area, just grazing the northern suburbs of Atlanta. Snowfall amounts north of a Rome to Gainesville line were mostly around 2.0 inches, but some counties in the northeast Georgia mountains reported 5.0 - 6.0 inch snowfall amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that about 0.10 inch of glaze ice was noted on elevated objects and trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the southeast United States during the early morning hours of Sunday, February 25th.  While no severe weather was observed or reported within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, a couple of lines of thunderstorms, with occasional lightning and heavy rain, moved through the area during the hours of 2 am and 10 am.  Two lightning-related fires resulted in west central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Newnan Times-Herald reported that lightning set a home on Bethlehem Church Road on fire.  The lightning actually struck an adjacent tree which caused the lightning to travel through a cable starting a fire in the garage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clarke county/Athens 911 center reported less than 1/4 inch glaze ice, less than 1/4 inch sleet, and just a trace of snow.  This event did not meet winter storm warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified Arctic air mass remained over the region during mid-February.  Meanwhile, a weak upper disturbance passed over the cold air.  This resulted in light precipitation, mostly in the form of light snow, across the far northern counties of Georgia.  Accumulations of snow were generally confined to areas north and east of an Ellijay, to Dawsonville, to Dahlonega line.  Much of the snow melted within a few hours of falling as temperatures remained near or just above the freezing mark throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fannin county 911 center reported around 1.0 inch of snow, which quickly melted after 1 am EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper shortwave was moving east-northeast from the Gulf of Mexico during the late afternoon and into the evening, with a moist and unstable air mass at the surface. At the same time, a 130 knot upper jet lifted northward from the Gulf of Mexico. This combination of moisture, instability, and strong wind fields aloft allowed for the development of organized severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 400 yard wide by 800 yard long swath of damage was caused by a downburst. Over 100 trees were uprooted or snapped off. Maximum wind speeds were estimated to be 105 mph. Skirting was torn off several mobile homes, a mobile home was pushed 2 feet off its foundation.  Shingles were torn of roofs from several homes, a pop up camper was flipped over and a satellite antenna was knocked over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of March 1 ahead of advancing warm front across southwest Georgia, producing several reports of wind damage.  Later that evening into the predawn hours of March 2, a squall line formed ahead of a cold front, with several reports of wind damage and tornadoes across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.  One of the tornadoes tore through a mobile home park just north of Newton, killing six and injuring three.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-1 tornado moved from northern Tift County into southeastern Turner County.  Many trees on T & C Circle along the Tift-Turner county line were snapped and laid along the fence on the rural road.  Roofs were peeled off large metal hay barns.  One large barn was destroyed with several others damaged.  A pivot irrigation system was damaged.  Fifteen homes were damaged, of which two were destroyed, ten with major damage, and three with moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Phasing of jet streams allowed for a rapidly developing low pressure system. Large scale dynamics and instability allowed for several low topped supercells to develop primarily along and ahead of a cold front over portions of western counties of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Harvey Wilson Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado touched down on Sumner Lakes Road just south of Sumner.  One mobile home was completely destroyed, with the debris blown several hundred yards away. Of the two dozen structures that were damaged,  half were heavily damaged.  Numerous utility poles and trees in the path of the tornado were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed on the back side of a strong low pressure system moving up the east coast.  This was in the wake of the major tornado outbreak which occurred on March 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post reported that strong wind gusts resulted in a the collapse of a home under construction on Shumard Oak Drive in a new phase of the Chateau Elan subdivision in western Barrow county near the Gwinnett county line.  All three workers had to be transported to a local hospital.  One worker in the home was seriously injured, while two others were treated for minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of March 1 ahead of advancing warm front across southwest Georgia, producing several reports of wind damage.  Later that evening into the predawn hours of March 2, a squall line formed ahead of a cold front, with several reports of wind damage and tornadoes across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.  One of the tornadoes tore through a mobile home park just north of Newton, killing six and injuring three.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-2 tornado moved northeast from Baker County into Mitchell County just west of Baconton.  GEMA and FEMA assessed 26 minor damaged homes, 25 major damaged homes, and two destroyed homes.  Thirteen businesses sustained minor losses.  Most of the damage occurred just north of Baconton on Gravel Hill Road in the Pleasant Grove community.  Over 200 acres of pecan trees were uprooted.  The tornado flipped over a semi on U.S. Highway 19 about four miles north of Baconton then crossed into southeastern Dougherty County.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of March 1 ahead of advancing warm front across southwest Georgia, producing several reports of wind damage.  Later that evening into the predawn hours of March 2, a squall line formed ahead of a cold front, with several reports of wind damage and tornadoes across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.  One of the tornadoes tore through a mobile home park just north of Newton, killing six and injuring three.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-2 tornado continued on its northeast track from northern Mitchell County into southeastern Dougherty County.  It ripped carports and roof shingles off several homes on County Line Road and Jenkins Road.  It also snapped hundreds of trees before it crossed into western Worth County.  Ten homes suffered minor damage and two were heavily damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of March 1 ahead of advancing warm front across southwest Georgia, producing several reports of wind damage.  Later that evening into the predawn hours of March 2, a squall line formed ahead of a cold front, with several reports of wind damage and tornadoes across portions of southwest and south central Georgia.  One of the tornadoes tore through a mobile home park just north of Newton, killing six and injuring three.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado, rated EF-2, was spawned from the same supercell thunderstorm that produced the earlier tornado in Worth County.  It touched down near McCarty Road.  A brick frame house sustained severe roof damage with a couple of exterior walls collapsing.  Two vehicles parked outside were carried into a nearby field.  Large oak trees were uprooted, with one falling on a house.  One mobile home was flipped over and rolled into a nearby tree.  The tornado then moved northeast into a forest and uprooted hundreds of pines.  It flattened a house, causing two minor injuries, before lifting just west of State Highway 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, unstable air mass developed across north Georgia in advance of a very strong, winterlike cold front.  The pattern evolved as an intense upper low depended over the eastern provinces of Canada.  The afternoon and evening of the 3rd was characterized by isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms, several of which took on the characteristics of super cells.  Large hail was the main severe weather concern during this time period.  Most of the activity was confined to areas north of Interstate-20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bartow County Emergency Management Director reported ping-pong ball or half-dollar-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County Emergency Management Director reported that between 30 and 40 trees were blown down near the Intersection of U.S. 129 and Collins Road, about five miles south of Cleveland.  In addition, a chicken house was destroyed in the same area.  The damage was determined to be straight-line wind damage.  Numerous other trees were down in the northeast part of the county northeast of Helen.  Georgia Highway 17 north of Helen was closed and blocked because of downed trees.

One of the tornadoes tore through a mobile home park just north of Newton, killing six and injuring three.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved from Clay County into Quitman County along Hatcher Road before it lifted.  Numerous trees were snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado touched down in rural Baker County west of Newton, then traveled rapidly northeast, and caused extensive damage to a mobile home park a mile north of Newton.  Six people died and three were injured when their mobile homes were demolished by the tornado.  A church was destroyed on State Highway 37 a few miles west of Newton.  GEMA and FEMA damage assessments determined ten minor damaged homes, nine major damaged homes, and 18 destroyed homes.  The tornado crossed into northern Mitchell County north of Baconton.  A state of emergency was declared by the Governor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Strong Upper Level Disturbance and an associated surface wave of low pressure accompanied by very cold temperatures aloft, sparked several severe thunderstorms across the region.
A Strong Upper Level Disturbance and an associated surface wave of low pressure accompanied by very cold temperatures aloft, sparked several severe thunderstorms across the region.

A significant disturbance in northwest flow event provided for scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the thunderstorms became severe, mainly producing large damaging hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Courier Herald of Dublin reported that lightning struck the Graham Memorial Church of the Nazarene.  Damage was confined to the steeple and inside water damage from heavy rain and fire fighting water hoses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant disturbance in northwest flow event provided for scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Several of the thunderstorms became severe, mainly producing large damaging hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Kylee Dawn Circle setting the attic on fire.  The attic sustained minor to moderate damage as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low and over the northeast U.S. and associated large Canadian surface high pressure area brought record cold to much of the eastern U.S. during the Easter weekend.  Temperatures averaged a good 20 degrees below normal during the period.  Freezing temperatures were observed each morning from the 7th through the 9th, but the 7th and 8th brought the most widespread and coldest temperatures to the region.  Lows in the 20s were common across much of north and central Georgia, with lower 20s in parts of the northeast Georgia mountains.  Average freeze dates for most of north and central Georgia are in late March.  Farmers in all of the 96 north and central Georgia counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were declared eligible for federal loans as a result of the unusually late hard freeze.  The unseasonably cold and freezing temperatures resulted in the loss of nearly 100 percent of the wine grape crop, 86 percent of the blueberry crop, 78 percent of the peach crop, 41 percent loss of grasses, 40 percent of grain loss (corn and wheat), and 29 percent loss of pecan crops.  Of these crops, wine grapes are nearly all grown in north Georgia and a substantial portion of the peach crop is grown in central Georgia, both within Peachtree City, Georgia's forecast area.  Total losses for the entire state as a result of the freeze were estimated at $258 million, of which 50 to 60 percent of this was estimated to be within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.

The strong upper trough moving through the Ohio Valley continued to cause problems in the southeast for yet another day.  As was the case on the 4th, severe thunderstorms were confined to the far southeast part of the County Warning Area (CWA).  Northwest flow aloft kept isolated thunderstorms going well after dark and into the early morning hours of the 6th.  Renewed, albeit isolated, thunderstorm development occurred in the west central part of the state during the early morning hours before sunrise on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail about three miles east-northeast of Swainsboro.  The Emanuel County Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail about 4.5 miles east of Swainsboro.  The public reported that nickel-sized hail fell for up to 10 minutes in Twin City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave was moving through the Tennessee Valley late on the 4th.  This combined with a sea breeze resulted in a couple of isolated severe thunderstorms in the southeast portions of central Georgia during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County Emergency Management Director reported that a very southwestward moving thunderstorm dumped hail for up to an hour across parts of north central and northwest Toombs county.  Hail was first reported three miles northeast of Vidalia around 720 pm EDT and ended about three miles southwest of Vidalia near the Montgomery county line around 830 pm EDT.  Hail ranged in size from golf balls northeast of Vidalia, to pennies in Vidalia, to half-dollars southwest of Vidalia near the Montgomery county line.

Total losses for the entire state as a result of the freeze were estimated at $258 million, of which 50 to 60 percent of this was estimated to be within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.

The fourth tornado outbreak of the year for the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, and the second major tornado outbreak for the year, occurred across the southern portion of the forecast area, or central Georgia.  A broad upper trough over the central U.S. was tracking eastward while a surface low deepened rapidly as it moved from northern Mississippi into eastern Virginia.  A strong low-level jet accompanied the system with 50-60 knot winds observed at 850mb across central and south Georgia late in the day on the 14th.  The low-level jet transported deep Gulf moisture northward into the region. Sunshine during the early part of the day destabilized the region sufficiently to allow for strong to severe supercell thunderstorms to track across central Georgia just south of a warm frontal boundary across north Georgia.  Damage surveys confirmed that nine tornadoes tracked across central Georgia, including many of the same areas that were affected during the major tornado outbreak on March 1st.  The was the second most significant tornado outbreak to impact the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area since the August 29, 2005 outbreak associated with Hurricane Katrinia.  The state insurance commissioner reported that at least 81 site-built homes, 28 mobile homes, and 10 businesses were damaged or destroyed during the event.  Damages to structures alone were near $5 million, with additional damages the result of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that yet another tornado touched down in Dodge county and traveled very near the city of Eastman once again.   This tornado was from a different complex of thunderstorms than the one that caused the first tornado earlier.  However, this was the same complex of thunderstorms that caused the tornado earlier in Crisp county.  This time the tornado touched down one mile south of downtown Eastman, traveling northeast.  The total path length was 4.5 miles with a maximum path width of 200 yards.  The maximum wind speed within the tornado was estimated to be 120 mph.  The Dodge County Road Department's garage was destroyed.  Two mobile homes in the area were destroyed.  A nearby home suffered significant damage when parts of its roof, an exterior wall, and patio were removed.  An adjacent barn was also destroyed.  Several other homes in the area sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia and damage reports received from the Hall Emergency Management Director, a cooperative observer, and the public confirmed that a microburst with straight-line winds of 70 to 80 mph with isolated damage of 100-110 mph caused considerable damage to a subdivision just east of Interstate-985 and just south of Flowery Branch.  The path of damage was approximately one-half mile long and a mile wide.  At least 35 homes sustained roof and siding damage and dozens of large trees were blown down or completely uprooted in the area.  One home was blown 15 feet off its foundation and destroyed as it collapsed.  Windows were blown out of at least one home and several vehicles.  Other vehicles were damaged from downed trees or debris.  One man was injured when his locked and bolted door blew open with the winds and knocked him into a nearby wall.  There were reports of other trees down in the southern part of the county, but especially in the far southern part of the county near the Gwinnett county line.  The majority of the damage was confined to Devonshire, Hillsdale, and Glenmore Drives.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 17th a wildfire broke out along Sweat Road, Ware County, Georgia when a power line fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On April 16th a wildfire broke out along Sweat Road, Ware County, Georgia when a power line fell. As of April 30th, wildfires had burned more than 66,000 acres (100 square miles) in southeast Georgia near the Okefenokee Swamp.  Flame lengths up to 100 feet were reported with this fire with strong winds and abnormally dry fuels. At this time Fire officials were calling it the largest fire on record for the state of Georgia.  Eighteen homes were destroyed by the fire up until this date.  The fire was apparently started when strong winds knocked a power line down, igniting tinder-dry trees.  The blaze was approximately 70% contained on April 30th.|Emergency officials temporarily closed a 15-20 mile stretch of U.S. Highway 1 and the CSX railroad track that runs along the highway.  Portions of the highway were still closed on 30 April.  Other state and local roads were closed due to reduced visibilities from smoke and ash.  Additionally, more than 1000 residents near Waycross, GA, were forced to evacuate.|Two NWS Incident Meteorologists (IMETs) were currently deployed to date at the fire. NWS WFO Jacksonville conducted daily briefings for the Ware County EOC and Executive Committee at 5 p.m.  The fires continued into May and June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper trough moving through the Ohio Valley continued to cause problems in the southeast for yet another day.  As was the case on the 4th, severe thunderstorms were confined to the far southeast part of the County Warning Area (CWA).  Northwest flow aloft kept isolated thunderstorms going well after dark and into the early morning hours of the 6th.  Renewed, albeit isolated, thunderstorm development occurred in the west central part of the state during the early morning hours before sunrise on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck three homes in the county.  Damage was minor and confined to electrical wiring damage.  No fires were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the entire state during May.  Rainfall deficits across many counties of north and central Georgia continued to grow as well as the number of counties classified in severe and extreme drought conditions.  By the end of May 2007, 74 Georgia counties were classified as being in extreme drought, 79 in severe drought, and six in moderate drought.  Counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area classified as being in extreme drought include Bartow, Carroll, Catoosa, Chattooga, Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, Coweta, Dade, DeKalb, Douglas, Fannin, Fayette, Floyd, Fulton, Gilmer, Gordon, Haralson, Harris, Heard, Meriwether, Murray, Paulding, Pickens, Polk, Towns, Troup, Union, Walker and Whitfield.  Drought conditions in all remaining counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were classified as severe.  Rainfall deficits as of May 31, 2007 for some of the major north and central Georgia cities were:||Athens:  6:49 inches|Columbus:  8.68 inches|Macon:  9.30 inches|Atlanta:  10.02 inches|Plains:  10.09 inches|Blairsville:  13.89 inches|LaFayette:  14.06 inches||Macon observed its driest May in history with only a trace of precipitation.||Record to near-record low stream flows were reported by the end of May across the state, including the Coosawattee River near Ellijay, the Oostanaula at Resaca and Rome, the Middle Oconee near Athens, Oconee at Milledgeville and Dublin, and the Ocmulgee near Jackson and at Lumber City.  Groundwater levels were dropping statewide.  Many wells are approaching their average yearly low water level, normally reached in late summer or early fall.   Much of the state remained under level-2 outdoor water restrictions, with total bans on outdoor watering in some west central Georgia counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Bay Wildfire Complex (The Sweat Farm Road Fire and Big Turnaround Fire) continued from April through the entire month of May.  These fires are located 10 miles south of Waycross, GA and 16 miles west of Folkston, Georgia in Ware and Charlton Counties, Georgia, and Baker County, Florida. By the end of the month the Sweat Farm Road Fire had burned 87,784 acres with 709 fire personnel working the fire and was 90 percent contained.  The Big Turnaround Fire had consumed 402,490 acres with 414 personnel working the fire and was 65 percent contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low and over the northeast U.S. and associated large Canadian surface high pressure area brought record cold to much of the eastern U.S. during the Easter weekend.  Temperatures averaged a good 20 degrees below normal during the period.  Freezing temperatures were observed each morning from the 7th through the 9th, but the 7th and 8th brought the most widespread and coldest temperatures to the region.  Lows in the 20s were common across much of north and central Georgia, with lower 20s in parts of the northeast Georgia mountains.  Average freeze dates for most of north and central Georgia are in late March.  Farmers in all of the 96 north and central Georgia counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were declared eligible for federal loans as a result of the unusually late hard freeze.  The unseasonably cold and freezing temperatures resulted in the loss of nearly 100 percent of the wine grape crop, 86 percent of the blueberry crop, 78 percent of the peach crop, 41 percent loss of grasses, 40 percent of grain loss (corn and wheat), and 29 percent loss of pecan crops.  Of these crops, wine grapes are nearly all grown in north Georgia and a substantial portion of the peach crop is grown in central Georgia, both within Peachtree City, Georgia's forecast area.  Total losses for the entire state as a result of the freeze were estimated at $258 million, of which 50 to 60 percent of this was estimated to be within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.

The unseasonably cold and freezing temperatures resulted in the loss of nearly 100 percent of the wine grape crop, 86 percent of the blueberry crop, 78 percent of the peach crop, 41 percent loss of grasses, 40 percent of grain loss (corn and wheat), and 29 percent loss of pecan crops.  Of these crops, wine grapes are nearly all grown in north Georgia and a substantial portion of the peach crop is grown in central Georgia, both within Peachtree City, Georgia's forecast area.  Total losses for the entire state as a result of the freeze were estimated at $258 million, of which 50 to 60 percent of this was estimated to be within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.

The fourth tornado outbreak of the year for the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, and the second major tornado outbreak for the year, occurred across the southern portion of the forecast area, or central Georgia.  A broad upper trough over the central U.S. was tracking eastward while a surface low deepened rapidly as it moved from northern Mississippi into eastern Virginia.  A strong low-level jet accompanied the system with 50-60 knot winds observed at 850mb across central and south Georgia late in the day on the 14th.  The low-level jet transported deep Gulf moisture northward into the region. Sunshine during the early part of the day destabilized the region sufficiently to allow for strong to severe supercell thunderstorms to track across central Georgia just south of a warm frontal boundary across north Georgia.  Damage surveys confirmed that nine tornadoes tracked across central Georgia, including many of the same areas that were affected during the major tornado outbreak on March 1st.  The was the second most significant tornado outbreak to impact the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area since the August 29, 2005 outbreak associated with Hurricane Katrinia.  The state insurance commissioner reported that at least 81 site-built homes, 28 mobile homes, and 10 businesses were damaged or destroyed during the event.  Damages to structures alone were near $5 million, with additional damages the result of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that the EF1 tornado that began in western Treutlen county near Orland, continued into the extreme western portion of Emanuel county just south of Adrian, and then continued into extreme southern Johnson county.  This was the first of two touchdowns of this tornado in Emanuel county as it tracked into the county again after moving out of Johnson county.  This portion of the tornado track was approximately one mile long with a maximum path width of 440 yards.  Most of the damage done by this portion of the tornado was within the city of Adrian, which sits right on the Emanuel/Johnson county line.  Here, one mobile home was rolled over and a portion of a roof was removed from a utility building.  Approximately a dozen homes within the city of Adrian received at least minor damage.  Numerous trees were downed in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that the EF1 tornado, which originated in extreme western Treutlen county and briefly crossed into Emanuel county, continued into southern Johnson county, causing considerable damage to the city of Adrian on the Johnson/Emanuel county line.  This tornado then continued to travel north-northeast through the extreme southeast corner of Johnson county before exiting once again into Emanuel county.  The EF1 tornado traveled approximately three miles within Johnson county with a maximum path width of 440 yards.  A good portion of the city of Adrian, the center of which lies in Emanuel county, lies within Johnson county.  All together, at least a dozen homes, some mobile homes, were damaged in the Adrian community.  A number of these were on the Johnson county side of the line.  Two homes in a campground just northeast of Adrian were heavily damaged from downed trees.  Numerous trees down throughout this area as a result of the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that the EF1 tornado, that originally began back in western Treutlen county near Orland, traveled into extreme western Emanuel county across the city of Adrian, then into far southeastern Johnson county, crossed back one final time into Emanuel county before lifting about three miles north-northeast of Adrian, or about five miles west of Norristown Junction in extreme southwest Emanuel county.  This portion of the track was approximately one mile long.  Since this portion of the tornado traveled across a largely rural, forested area, damage was confined mostly to downed trees.  However, one unsecured mobile home in this portion of the county was destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that a tornado, which originated in Worth county (covered by National Weather Service Tallahassee, Florida) tracked into Crisp county.  The tornado entered Crisp county approximately 5.5 miles west of Arabi and traveled northeast lifting about three miles northeast of Arabi.  The overall path length of the tornado, which inculdes both the Worth and Crisp county portions of the track, was 14 miles, approximately 7.5 miles of which occurred within Crisp county.  The maximum path width was 200 yards.  Maximum winds within the tornado were estimated at 100 mph.  A mobile home was shifted off its foundation near Arabi and the support straps were pulled out of the ground.  A number of trees were down in the area, some of which were blocking roads.  The tornado tracked across Interstate-75 and a vehicle was tossed into the guard rail near exit 92.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of hail, ranging in size from quarters to half-dollars, were received from the public across Cobb county. Quarter-sized hail was reported at most locations, but half-dollar-sized hail was reported from west central Cobb county, about six miles southwest of Kennesaw near Lost Mountain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An aerial and ground survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that the second tornado that originated in Laurens county five miles southeast of Dublin tracked northeast to east-northeast across northeast Laurens county into Johnson county, approximately three miles north of Kite, and then continued on an east-northeastward track lifting about five miles southwest of Kite.  The overall tornado path length was 19 miles, of which approxmiately five miles fell within Johnson county.  The maximum tornado path length was 100 yards with maximum winds estimated at 100 mph.  The majority of the damage along this portion of the tornado path occurred just north of Scott, where several homes sustained minor to moderate damage.  A church in this area sustained minor damage and a family life center suffered considerable damage when it was shifted slightly off its foundation.  Elsewhere along the tornado path within Johnson county, damage was confined mostly to trees and some power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of hail, ranging in size from nickels to golf balls, were received from the Forsyth County Emergency Management Director and the public across northern Forsyth county from west of Matt, along Matt Highway near the Cherokee county line, to Silver City on the Dawson county line, and eastward to northeast of Chestatee in the extreme northeast corner of the county.  Large hail was reported in Matt, Coal Mountain and Silver City.  The Emergency Manager reported golf ball-sized hail near the Cherokee county line, west of Matt on Georgia Highway.  Several automobiles in the area sustained broken windshields.  Golf ball-sized hail was also reported from five miles south to three miles east of Silver City in extreme northern Forsyth county.  There were other reports from the public of quarter-sized hail from Matt to just south of Silver City.  The public also observed nickel-sized hail further south, just north of Cumming.  Near Silver City, the public reported that hail, the size of ping pong balls or larger, fell for up to 10 minutes and the ground was covered in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated multicell and a few supercell thunderstorms formed during the evening hours as a warm front moved into the area and an unseasonably cold air mass, which had been over the region for nearly a week, retreated to the northeast.  Because the surface air mass was not particularly warm or moist, severe weather was mostly confined to penny and nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather observer for the Fort Benning military reservation observed half-dollar to golf ball-sized hail.  The public also observed golf ball-sized hail in the area as well.  The Chattahoochee County 911 Center reported quarter-sized hail in Cusseta.  The weather observer at Fort Benning also observed wind gusts to 43 mph.  Some small tree limbs were blown down in the area.

Farmers in all of the 96 north and central Georgia counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were declared eligible for federal loans as a result of the unusually late hard freeze.  The unseasonably cold and freezing temperatures resulted in the loss of nearly 100 percent of the wine grape crop, 86 percent of the blueberry crop, 78 percent of the peach crop, 41 percent loss of grasses, 40 percent of grain loss (corn and wheat), and 29 percent loss of pecan crops.  Of these crops, wine grapes are nearly all grown in north Georgia and a substantial portion of the peach crop is grown in central Georgia, both within Peachtree City, Georgia's forecast area.  Total losses for the entire state as a result of the freeze were estimated at $258 million, of which 50 to 60 percent of this was estimated to be within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.

The fourth tornado outbreak of the year for the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, and the second major tornado outbreak for the year, occurred across the southern portion of the forecast area, or central Georgia.  A broad upper trough over the central U.S. was tracking eastward while a surface low deepened rapidly as it moved from northern Mississippi into eastern Virginia.  A strong low-level jet accompanied the system with 50-60 knot winds observed at 850mb across central and south Georgia late in the day on the 14th.  The low-level jet transported deep Gulf moisture northward into the region. Sunshine during the early part of the day destabilized the region sufficiently to allow for strong to severe supercell thunderstorms to track across central Georgia just south of a warm frontal boundary across north Georgia.  Damage surveys confirmed that nine tornadoes tracked across central Georgia, including many of the same areas that were affected during the major tornado outbreak on March 1st.  The was the second most significant tornado outbreak to impact the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area since the August 29, 2005 outbreak associated with Hurricane Katrinia.  The state insurance commissioner reported that at least 81 site-built homes, 28 mobile homes, and 10 businesses were damaged or destroyed during the event.  Damages to structures alone were near $5 million, with additional damages the result of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An areal and ground damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that yet another tornado struck the eastern and northeast portion of Laurens county.  This was the third tornado to touch down in Laurens county within an hour.  It is believed that this tornado originated from the same thunderstorm that produced the tornado across central Laurens county a little earlier, which caused three injuries just south of Dublin.  This tornado was rated an EF1.  The track began approximately five miles southeast of Dublin and tracked northeast to east-northeast across the northeast portion of the county and crossed into Johnson county about eight miles east of Brewton.  The tornado then continued well into Johnson county, lifting about five miles southwest of Kite.  Fourteen miles of the approximately 19 mile long intermittent tornado path was within Laurens county.  The maximum path width was 100 yards.  The maximum wind speed in the tornado was estimated to be 100 mph.  Several outbuildings along the path of the tornado were either damaged or destroyed, but the most significant structural damage with this tornado occurred in Johnson county.  Several trees were blown down along the path of the tornado.

An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSB Television of Atlanta reported that several trees were down in the far northern portion of Fulton county between Crabapple and Birmingham.  Some power lines were also down in the area.  Trees were down on Hickory Road, Birmingham Road, Autry Mill, and Dill Mill Roads.  The Milton Herald reported that trees were also down on Medlock Bridge Road, causing it to be blocked and closed.

Lows in the 20s were common across much of north and central Georgia, with lower 20s in parts of the northeast Georgia mountains.  Average freeze dates for most of north and central Georgia are in late March.  Farmers in all of the 96 north and central Georgia counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were declared eligible for federal loans as a result of the unusually late hard freeze.  The unseasonably cold and freezing temperatures resulted in the loss of nearly 100 percent of the wine grape crop, 86 percent of the blueberry crop, 78 percent of the peach crop, 41 percent loss of grasses, 40 percent of grain loss (corn and wheat), and 29 percent loss of pecan crops.  Of these crops, wine grapes are nearly all grown in north Georgia and a substantial portion of the peach crop is grown in central Georgia, both within Peachtree City, Georgia's forecast area.  Total losses for the entire state as a result of the freeze were estimated at $258 million, of which 50 to 60 percent of this was estimated to be within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.

The fourth tornado outbreak of the year for the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, and the second major tornado outbreak for the year, occurred across the southern portion of the forecast area, or central Georgia.  A broad upper trough over the central U.S. was tracking eastward while a surface low deepened rapidly as it moved from northern Mississippi into eastern Virginia.  A strong low-level jet accompanied the system with 50-60 knot winds observed at 850mb across central and south Georgia late in the day on the 14th.  The low-level jet transported deep Gulf moisture northward into the region. Sunshine during the early part of the day destabilized the region sufficiently to allow for strong to severe supercell thunderstorms to track across central Georgia just south of a warm frontal boundary across north Georgia.  Damage surveys confirmed that nine tornadoes tracked across central Georgia, including many of the same areas that were affected during the major tornado outbreak on March 1st.  The was the second most significant tornado outbreak to impact the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area since the August 29, 2005 outbreak associated with Hurricane Katrinia.  The state insurance commissioner reported that at least 81 site-built homes, 28 mobile homes, and 10 businesses were damaged or destroyed during the event.  Damages to structures alone were near $5 million, with additional damages the result of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County Emergency Management Director reported that three trees were down in the northern part of the county.  One large pine tree had been blown down onto New Normantown Road, one tree was down on a nearby farm, and another tree was down on Van Lewis Road, all between Vidalia and Normantown.

A very warm, unstable air mass developed across north Georgia in advance of a very strong, winterlike cold front.  The pattern evolved as an intense upper low depended over the eastern provinces of Canada.  The afternoon and evening of the 3rd was characterized by isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms, several of which took on the characteristics of super cells.  Large hail was the main severe weather concern during this time period.  Most of the activity was confined to areas north of Interstate-20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported penny-sized hail at the corner of Macland and Powder Springs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The fourth tornado outbreak of the year for the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, and the second major tornado outbreak for the year, occurred across the southern portion of the forecast area, or central Georgia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, GA confirmed that an EF2 tornado had touched down in central Laurens county, about 8.5 miles south-southwest of Dublin, or just south of Garetta, near the intersection of U.S. Highway 441/319 and Georgia Highway 117.  The tornado traveled east-northeast across Turkey Creek Church Road, lifting approximately 5.5 miles south-southeast of Dublin near the Oconee River.  The tornado had a path length of approximately six miles and a maximum path width of 200 yards.  Maximum wind speeds were estimated at 120 mph.  Most of the damage caused by the tornado was along Turkey Creek Church Road between U.S. Highway 441 and the Oconee River.  A tied-down double-wide mobile home was completely destroyed along Turkey Creek Church Road resulting in two injuries.  Another tied-down mobile home was destroyed in the same area with two adults and two children inside.  All occupants survived, but one sustained minor injuries.  The carport of a site-built home was removed as well as a portion of the roof.  Several other mobile homes and site-built homes sustained minor damage from wind and fallen trees.  A large greenhouse was also destroyed.  A number of trees and power lines were down all along the path of the tornado.  A few trees were also down just prior to the path of the tornado near Rentz and Cadwell.

Several car windshields were broken from large hail on Georgia Highway 100 north of Cave Spring.  Further east in the southeast part of the county, southeast of Lindale, there were widespread reports from the public of penny to nickel-sized hail.

Farmers in all of the 96 north and central Georgia counties within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area were declared eligible for federal loans as a result of the unusually late hard freeze.  The unseasonably cold and freezing temperatures resulted in the loss of nearly 100 percent of the wine grape crop, 86 percent of the blueberry crop, 78 percent of the peach crop, 41 percent loss of grasses, 40 percent of grain loss (corn and wheat), and 29 percent loss of pecan crops.  Of these crops, wine grapes are nearly all grown in north Georgia and a substantial portion of the peach crop is grown in central Georgia, both within Peachtree City, Georgia's forecast area.  Total losses for the entire state as a result of the freeze were estimated at $258 million, of which 50 to 60 percent of this was estimated to be within the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area.

A deep upper low and over the northeast U.S. and associated large Canadian surface high pressure area brought record cold to much of the eastern U.S. during the Easter weekend. The fourth tornado outbreak of the year for the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area, and the second major tornado outbreak for the year, occurred across the southern portion of the forecast area, or central Georgia.  A broad upper trough over the central U.S. was tracking eastward while a surface low deepened rapidly as it moved from northern Mississippi into eastern Virginia.  A strong low-level jet accompanied the system with 50-60 knot winds observed at 850mb across central and south Georgia late in the day on the 14th.  The low-level jet transported deep Gulf moisture northward into the region. Sunshine during the early part of the day destabilized the region sufficiently to allow for strong to severe supercell thunderstorms to track across central Georgia just south of a warm frontal boundary across north Georgia.  Damage surveys confirmed that nine tornadoes tracked across central Georgia, including many of the same areas that were affected during the major tornado outbreak on March 1st.  The was the second most significant tornado outbreak to impact the Peachtree City, Georgia forecast area since the August 29, 2005 outbreak associated with Hurricane Katrinia.  The state insurance commissioner reported that at least 81 site-built homes, 28 mobile homes, and 10 businesses were damaged or destroyed during the event.  Damages to structures alone were near $5 million, with additional damages the result of downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that an EF1 tornado, with a maximum wind speed of 90 mph, had touched down about one mile west of downtown Eastman and traveled north-northeast to a point about four miles north-northeast of downtown Eastman.  The total path length of the tornado was 4.5 miles with a maximum path width of 100 yards.  A number of retail buildings on U.S. Highway 23 sustained minor damage.  However, an adjacent greenhouse was destroyed.  The announcers booth at the Eastman High School football field was destroyed.  Two unsecured mobile homes along the path of the tornado were destroyed.  A man and woman were injured when a home in the the Greenwood Heights Subdivision was destroyed.  Both injuries were the result of fallen and flying debris.  The home was destroyed by a large tree which crashed through the home.  Numerous trees and power lines were downed along the path of the tornado, several of which fell on and caused damage to homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service office in Peachtree City, Georgia confirmed that yet a second tornado had touched down in southern Laurens county.  The tornado occurred almost simultaneously with the first tornado that tracked just south of Dublin.  This tornado touched down about 4.0 miles north-northeast of the town of Cedar Grove and tracked east-northeast lifting about 1.5 miles north-northeast of Lowrey in southeast Laurens county.  The total path length of the tornado was approximately six miles with a maximum path width of 50 yards.  The maximum determined wind speed was 80 mph.  Numerous trees were downed along the path of the tornado, which tracked through a mostly forested area.  However, a few outbuildings were damaged near Mortan and Rock Springs Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ground and aerial damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Peachtree City, Georgia, confirmed that an EF1 tornado touched down in western Treutlen county, near the Laurens county line, about 2 miles southeast of Rockledge or 1 mile south-southeast of Orland, just south of Interstate-16.  The tornado traveled on a north-northeastward path across the town of Orianna, along the Laurens county line, continued north-northeast into the far western part of Emanuel county, and eventually into southern Johnson county across the town of Adrian, ending three miles north-northeast of Adrian, one again in extreme western Emanuel county.  The overall tornado path length was approximately 10 miles, seven of which were in Treutlen county.  The maximum path width was one-quarter mile wide.  Maximum winds speeds were estimated at 100 mph.  Much of the damage caused by the tornado in Truetlen county occurred in the community of Orianna where two homes were heavily damaged and two outbuildings were destroyed.  Another home northeast of Orianna sustained minor damage.  A grain silo was blown one-quarter mile away from its original location.  Hundreds of trees were blown down along and just north of Interstate-16 near the initial touchdown point of the tornado.

Upper shortwave was moving east-northeast from the Gulf of Mexico during the late afternoon and into the evening, with a moist and unstable air mass at the surface. At the same time, a 130 knot upper jet lifted northward from the Gulf of Mexico. This combination of moisture, instability, and strong wind fields aloft allowed for the development of organized severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed 2 miles east of Sylvania and traveled north-northeast about 6/10th of a mile before lifting. The maximum width was 35 yards, and the maximum winds were near 105 mph. This places the tornado in the upper end of the F1 category. This tornado destroyed a pump house, knocked a large tree branch onto a car, damaged a mobile home, an outbuilding, and uprooted and snapped several trees.  Nearly a dozen large pecan trees were uprooted.  Other pecan trees were damaged with large limbs broken off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed at the intersection of the differential heating boundary and the sea breeze front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of power lines down in the city of Millen.  A 5 foot diameter tree fell on a trailer near the Jenkins County Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated multicell and a few supercell thunderstorms formed during the evening hours as a warm front moved into the area and an unseasonably cold air mass, which had been over the region for nearly a week, retreated to the northeast.  Because the surface air mass was not particularly warm or moist, severe weather was mostly confined to penny and nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow, a result of an unseasonably deep polar vortex over the northeast U.S. and southeast Canada, combined with several short waves embedded within the northwest flow aloft, to lead to a two-day outbreak of severe thunderstorms.  This was the most significant bout of severe weather north and central Georgia had experienced since mid-April with an almost complete lack of severe weather in May, normally a very active month.  The activity peaked during the afternoon and evening hours, but lingered well overnight on the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th.  Most of these thunderstorms affected central and east central Georgia.  More widespread severe thunderstorms were observed on the 12th, including much of the Atlanta metropolitan area.  A number of the storms produced large hail, while a lesser amount produced damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Putnam County Emergency Management Director reported that up to a dozen trees were down in the far northeast part of the county, about 10 miles northeast of Eatonton.  Several of the trees were down on roads and several others were down on power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spalding County 911 Center reported that a number of trees and power lines were down from Sunny Side to the west side of Griffin.  Trees were down on Manley Road west of Atlanta Road in the north part of the county, near Sunny Side.  The Griffin Daily News reported that the downed trees also caused several power lines to be knocked down.  Power lines were down across Manley Road resulting in several vehicle accidents.  The public reported minor damage to a home in Griffin.  A few shingles, parts of a gutter and siding were damaged.  Loose items, such as lawn furniture, were blown from one yard into another.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave in westerly flow aloft brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 19th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow, a result of an unseasonably deep polar vortex over the northeast U.S. and southeast Canada, combined with several short waves embedded within the northwest flow aloft, to lead to a two-day outbreak of severe thunderstorms.  This was the most significant bout of severe weather north and central Georgia had experienced since mid-April with an almost complete lack of severe weather in May, normally a very active month.  The activity peaked during the afternoon and evening hours, but lingered well overnight on the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th.  Most of these thunderstorms affected central and east central Georgia.  More widespread severe thunderstorms were observed on the 12th, including much of the Atlanta metropolitan area.  A number of the storms produced large hail, while a lesser amount produced damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported golf ball-sized hail in the southwest part of Taliaferro county near the Hancock county line and southwest of Crawfordville.

Thunderstorms also developed during the afternoon of the 9th as well.  However, the only incident reported on the 9th was a lightning strike.

Penny-sized hail was reported five miles northwest of Canton, or one mile southeast of Waleska by a trained spotter.  Pea-sized hail was reported further east about seven miles east of Waleska.  In addition, the Emergency Management Director measured a wind gust of 47 mph in Waleska during the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extended period of dry conditions, a widespread thunderstorm event during the afternoon and early evening hours brought welcome rain to the area, but unfortunately a number of severe thunderstorms as well.  The thunderstorms were fueled by a stationary front lying across central Georgia combined with a short wave in northwest flow aloft as a large trough developed across the eastern U.S.  Most of the events were characterized by marginal severe thunderstorm wind and hail events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockdale Citizen reported that lightning struck and set on fire a detached garage to a home on Pleasant Hill Road.  The garage contained several antique vehicles.  The owner managed to get three of the vehicles out of the garage.  The remaining vehicles and the garage itself were a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave aloft, combined with daytime heating, brought scattered strong to severe thunderstorms to much of north and central Georgia.  Only isolated storms reached severe limits.  Several discrete lines of strong to severe thunderstorms were observed from mid-afternoon through the early evening hours on the 8th.  Thunderstorms also developed during the afternoon of the 9th as well.  However, the only incident reported on the 9th was a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed penny-sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County 911 Center reported that trees were down on power lines in at least 4 locations around the county from Villa Rica in the northeast to along Georgia Highway 5 in the southeast near Lowell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave aloft, combined with daytime heating, brought scattered strong to severe thunderstorms to much of north and central Georgia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockdale Citizen reported that lightning struck and set on fire a mobile home on Pine Lake Court in Lakeview Estates.  The family of seven was able to escape without injury.  However, the home and its contents were a total loss.  The family dog also perished in the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow, a result of an unseasonably deep polar vortex over the northeast U.S. and southeast Canada, combined with several short waves embedded within the northwest flow aloft, to lead to a two-day outbreak of severe thunderstorms.  This was the most significant bout of severe weather north and central Georgia had experienced since mid-April with an almost complete lack of severe weather in May, normally a very active month.  The activity peaked during the afternoon and evening hours, but lingered well overnight on the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th.  Most of these thunderstorms affected central and east central Georgia.  More widespread severe thunderstorms were observed on the 12th, including much of the Atlanta metropolitan area.  A number of the storms produced large hail, while a lesser amount produced damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dawson County 911 Center reported that penny-sized hail was observed on Georgia Highway 400 and Georgia Highway 53 near Dougherty and southward toward the Forsyth county line.  Pea-sized hail was observed in the Dawsonville area.

A number of the storms produced large hail, while a lesser amount produced damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County 911 Center and the public reported mostly penny-sized hail near the Dawson county on Georgia Highway 400 and at other locations in northeast Forsyth county from Silver City to Oscarville.  One report of quarter-sized hail was received.  One tree was down on Waldruf Road in the northeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong subtropical upper ridge continued over the area.  However, weak disturbances riding around the edges of the upper high combined with strong instability to result in considerable afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.  A number of these became severe producing hail and damaging winds.  Lightning was particularly significant with this event with a number of lightning-related structural fires reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County 911 Center reported that several trees were down across the southern and eastern part of the county.  Two power lines were also down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extended period of dry conditions, a widespread thunderstorm event during the afternoon and early evening hours brought welcome rain to the area, but unfortunately a number of severe thunderstorms as well.  The thunderstorms were fueled by a stationary front lying across central Georgia combined with a short wave in northwest flow aloft as a large trough developed across the eastern U.S.  Most of the events were characterized by marginal severe thunderstorm wind and hail events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Daily Post of Lawrenceville reported that lightning struck a home on Willowwind Drive on the Gwinnett county side of Loganville near the Walton county line.  The bonus room over the garage was set on fire.  A neighbor attempted to investigate the situation but was overcome by smoke inhalation and had to be treated at a local hospital as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coweta County 911 Center reported that a couple of trees were blown down on power lines along Belt Road near Sargent.  Winds were estimated by the public and storm spotters to be in the 45 to 50 mph range in Sharpsburg and Newnan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave in westerly flow aloft brought widespread showers and thunderstorms to north and central Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours of the 19th.  While most of the storms remained below severe limits, a couple produced isolated wind damage, including one incident in Atlanta that resulted in an injury when a tree fell on a vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton County 911 Center reported that a couple of power lines were down at the intersection of Georgia Highways 85 and 138.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The LaGrange Daily News reported that lightning struck a transformer outside the Dunson United Methodist Church on Barnard Avenue.  The lightning traveled through the electrical lines into one of the air conditioners in the crawl space resulting in a fire which caused considerable damage to the structure.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that strong thunderstorm winds blew down a power line and also broke off a railroad crossing arm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 25th proved to be one of the more significant severe weather days during the month.  This was the result of a strong short wave in westerly flow aloft and a very unstable and moist atmosphere present over the area.  Damaging downburst winds, large hail, and most significantly a number of lightning incidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported penny-sized hail in the Cumming area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated multicell and a few supercell thunderstorms formed during the evening hours as a warm front moved into the area and an unseasonably cold air mass, which had been over the region for nearly a week, retreated to the northeast.  Because the surface air mass was not particularly warm or moist, severe weather was mostly confined to penny and nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Morgan county sheriff's deputy observed penny-sized hail in the area of Interstate-20 and U.S. Highway 129 or Eatonton Road.  However, most of the hail observed in the area was pea-sized.

In McDonough, along Georgia Highway 155, hail covered the ground over one inch deep and a chain link fence was said to have been dented and damaged from the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary associated with a convective complex over the Atlantic waters moved inland during the late afternoon. This boundary as well as the sea breeze fronts produced numerous severe storms across the forecast area in a high moisture and high instability environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the Argyle area. Around the same time, a spotter reported mobile homes were pushed off of their foundations (about 4 inches) 3.5 miles north-northwest of Argyle.

Several of the trees were four to five feet in diameter.  One home sustained significant damage when a large oak tree fell on the structure.  The path of wind damage covered an area six to seven miles long in the far southwest and south central part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dawson County Emergency Management Director reported that a squall line of thunderstorms with strong straight-line winds had caused considerable damage in the northeast part of the county, northeast of Juno near the Dawson/Lumpkin county line.  Numerous large trees were down in the area and several structures and property in the area had received minor to moderate damage.  Most of the damage was confined to Greenway Road where three homes sustained minor wind damage, mostly in the form of damage of siding, decks, and fences.  A number of large trees were also down in these same areas causing additional damage to property.  A couple of work shops were also damaged in the same area when portions of tin roofs were blown off the structures in addition to siding damage.  Power lines and power poles were also down.  Outside of this area, the public and the Dawson County Emergency Management Director reported that trees were down in other scattered areas about the county, but most notably in the southern part of the county near the Forsyth county line along Cowart and Kelly Bridge Roads.  Greenway, Cowart, and Kelly Bridge roads all had to be closed for several hours because of numerous downed trees on the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an intense, but relatively brief high wind event affected the mountains and foothills on the evening of the 15th, another widespread damaging  high wind event developed during the day of the 16th.  Thousands of trees fell across the area, resulting in widespread power outages.  Numerous trees fell on roads, homes, and vehicles.

After an intense, but relatively brief high wind event affected the mountains and foothills on the evening of the 15th, another widespread damaging  high wind event developed during the day of the 16th.  Thousands of trees fell across the area, resulting in widespread power outages.  Numerous trees fell on roads, homes, and vehicles.

An outflow boundary from a large MCS that developed over Georgia pushed into our Georgia zones during the early morning hours.  In addition, an upper level short wave trough enhanced convection along the outflow. During the afternoon, the lingering outflow and sea breezes spawned more storms in a moist and unstable airmass over our Georgia zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Douglas, and one fell on a mobile home.

Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heard County Fire Chief reported that 15 to 20 trees were snapped off at the intersection of U.S. Highway 27 and West Ferry Road just north of Centralhatchee.  A recreational vehicle at the Overnight RV Park was blown over causing injury to a woman inside the vehicle.  She sustained cuts and scrapes when the vehicle was rolled over.  In addition, a wooden shed was also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow, a result of an unseasonably deep polar vortex over the northeast U.S. and southeast Canada, combined with several short waves embedded within the northwest flow aloft, to lead to a two-day outbreak of severe thunderstorms.  This was the most significant bout of severe weather north and central Georgia had experienced since mid-April with an almost complete lack of severe weather in May, normally a very active month.  The activity peaked during the afternoon and evening hours, but lingered well overnight on the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th.  Most of these thunderstorms affected central and east central Georgia.  More widespread severe thunderstorms were observed on the 12th, including much of the Atlanta metropolitan area.  A number of the storms produced large hail, while a lesser amount produced damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported half-dollar-sized hail in Temple for up to 10 minutes.  The Temple Fire Department observed quarter-sized hail two miles east of Temple.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow, a result of an unseasonably deep polar vortex over the northeast U.S. and southeast Canada, combined with several short waves embedded within the northwest flow aloft, to lead to a two-day outbreak of severe thunderstorms.  This was the most significant bout of severe weather north and central Georgia had experienced since mid-April with an almost complete lack of severe weather in May, normally a very active month.  The activity peaked during the afternoon and evening hours, but lingered well overnight on the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th.  Most of these thunderstorms affected central and east central Georgia.  More widespread severe thunderstorms were observed on the 12th, including much of the Atlanta metropolitan area.  A number of the storms produced large hail, while a lesser amount produced damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that quarter-sized hail fell for several minutes in the Harrison area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface heating in combination with steep low and mid level lapse rates in advance of an approaching shortwave trough, allowed for numerous thunderstorms to develop across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was reported down on Burton Ferry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extended period of dry conditions, a widespread thunderstorm event during the afternoon and early evening hours brought welcome rain to the area, but unfortunately a number of severe thunderstorms as well.  The thunderstorms were fueled by a stationary front lying across central Georgia combined with a short wave in northwest flow aloft as a large trough developed across the eastern U.S.  Most of the events were characterized by marginal severe thunderstorm wind and hail events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter and the Troup County 911 Center reported that two to three trees were blown down.  One tree was down along U.S. Highway 29 county of Hogansville, while at least two more trees were down along Georgia Highway 54 near the Meriwether county line.

The persistent drought continued to cause hardships to agricultural interests that were still recuperating from the April freeze. Dollar values for the drought damage should be included in either the August or September Storm Data for this region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface trough and sea breezes merged in a highly unstable airmass and spawned scattered severe storms across south Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail and wind gusts around 60 mph just northeast of K'ville, just southwest of Odum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm deposited large hail northeast of Georgetown, Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three field workers taking shelter under a pecan tree were struck by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three men taking shelter under a pecan tree off Ty Ty-Sparkss Road were struck by lightning.  Two of the men were injured and one was killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms produced wet microbursts with several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were down throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large upper low over the northeastern U.S. continued and was actually sinking a little further south toward the mid-Atlantic.  As a result, scattered thunderstorms developed once again during the heat of the afternoon from northerly flow and cold air aloft.  A few became severe producing marginal severe hail and downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Polk County Emergency Management Department observed quarter-sized hail southwest of Fish Creek and along Antioch Road near Antioch.  The 911 Center also reported that a tree had fallen on a power line at the intersection of Young's Farm Road and U.S. Highway 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 25th proved to be one of the more significant severe weather days during the month.  This was the result of a strong short wave in westerly flow aloft and a very unstable and moist atmosphere present over the area.  Damaging downburst winds, large hail, and most significantly a number of lightning incidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee of the Jackson Electric Membership Cooperative, which provides power to this local area, observed penny-sized hail across the eastern part of the county north of Candler.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell for 10 minutes.  The hail covered the ground and damaged tomato plants in a garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring hard freeze saw temperatures fall to the upper teens and lower 20s across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia on the morning of the 8th. This resulted in massive agricultural losses across the region. It was estimated that 90 percent of the apple and peach crop across the area was destroyed. As much as 50 percent of the berry crop was lost.
