EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the Terrell-Lee county line on State Highway 32.  Fifteen structures were damaged.  A mobile home and frame home were destroyed.  One woman was trapped in the bathroom of her home but was not injured.  Numerous trees and power lines were down.  Nearly 1,000 homes lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough was moving through the eastern half of the U.S. on the 26th.  Scattered thunderstorms accompanied this system across north Georgia during the afternoon hours.  A couple of these thunderstorms became strong to briefly severe across north central Georgia during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that a couple of trees and power lines were down in a subdivision north of Canton.  One of the downed trees caused some minor structural damage to a home in the subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced straight-line wind damage and three tornadoes across portions of southwest and south central Georgia during the evening and late night hours of April 14-15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down in the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced straight-line wind damage and three tornadoes across portions of southwest and south central Georgia during the evening and late night hours of April 14-15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado briefly touched down just north of Morgan.  It damaged a structure and felled numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, unstable air mass developed across north Georgia in advance of a very strong, winterlike cold front.  The pattern evolved as an intense upper low depended over the eastern provinces of Canada.  The afternoon and evening of the 3rd was characterized by isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms, several of which took on the characteristics of super cells.  Large hail was the main severe weather concern during this time period.  Most of the activity was confined to areas north of Interstate-20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed ping pong-ball-sized hail near the Forsyth county line, from just north of Silver City to three miles south-southeast of Dougherty.  Hail was observed on Georgia 400 just south of Dougherty.

The pattern evolved as an intense upper low depended over the eastern provinces of Canada.  The afternoon and evening of the 3rd was characterized by isolated to scattered severe thunderstorms, several of which took on the characteristics of super cells.  Large hail was the main severe weather concern during this time period.  Most of the activity was confined to areas north of Interstate-20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed quarter-sized hail in the Buckhead area northeast of Atlanta and an amateur radio operator reported penny-sized hail near the Cobb county line, or about one mile southeast of the intersection of Interstate-285 and Interstate-75.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong cold front was moving rapidly southeast through the eastern states early on the 4th.  This was supported by a very deep polar low intensifying across southeast Canada.  Very warm, humid, and unstable conditions continued in advance of this winterlike cold front.  A squall line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 3rd from the Ohio Valley into the Tennessee Valley.  This squall line continued to race out in advance of the front, reaching far north Georgia around midnight EDT and continued further south into central Georgia as a broken squall line during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Considerable wind damage occurred in association with the squall line across some north Georgia counties.  Several dozen homes were damaged in Hall county, including one blown off its foundation.  Other significant wind damage was noted in Dade and several other north Georgia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dade County Emergency Management Director reported that strong straight-line winds caused considerable damage between Trenton and Wildwoon.  One home lost part of its roof and a number of trees were also blown down on the property.  Four other structures were damaged along Hales Gap Road and near the Slygo Gap exit on Interstate-59.  One home was completely lifted off its foundation by the strong winds.  In addition, the steeple was blown off a church in the area.  Several trees were also down in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated multicell and a few supercell thunderstorms formed during the evening hours as a warm front moved into the area and an unseasonably cold air mass, which had been over the region for nearly a week, retreated to the northeast.  Because the surface air mass was not particularly warm or moist, severe weather was mostly confined to penny and nickel-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troup County 911 Center observed penny-sized hail in LaGrange.  The public reported penny-sized hail in West Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced straight-line wind damage and three tornadoes across portions of southwest and south central Georgia during the evening and late night hours of April 14-15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous downed trees were reported throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Non-convective wind gusts during the early afternoon of April 15, 2007, produced widespread tree and power line damage across portions of southeast Georgia following the passage of a strong front and squall line earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several docks destroyed or sunk along the Back River near Brunswick, Ga. Other dock damage reported across the area with two houseboats pushed against each other in the marina with $6,700 dollars in damage. Numerous tree limbs down across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on powerlines in Dupont and a tree down was blown down on carport on Virginia Ave in Homerville around 15:32.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down south of Warwick and traveled northeast.  Along its path, numerous downed trees and power lines blocked several roads including State Road 313.  Also, a home and several silos were damaged.  The tornado then crossed from northern Worth County into southwestern Crisp County (see Storm Data for Georgia, Central, for more information).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was firmly entrenched over the area keeping a very hot air mass in place.  Many areas across north and central Georgia had high temperatures in the 100-105 degree range, with a number of record high temperatures recorded.  This combined with a weak cold front draped across north Georgia to result in the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across mainly central Georgia.  The intense heat allowed the storms to linger well past sunset, even into the early morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a home starting a fire in the electrical system.  The home sustained at least minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door cold front was approaching north and east Georgia from the Carolinas.  Meanwhile...aloft a large upper high remained centered just west of the area.  These features combined with hot afternoon temperatures and a moist low-level air mass to result in scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  The activity was somewhat more widespread, more intense, and further north than had been observed on several previous days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public observed golf ball-sized hail in east central Carroll county, northeast of Carrollton, and nickel-sized hail in Villa Rica.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director reported that a thunderstorm downburst or microburst caused considerable damage across Johnson county.  The thunderstorm travelled from north to south, with the strongest winds observed in the western and central part of the county.  Several large trees were down on Georgia Highway 57 at Jim Underwood Road and several power lines were down as well.  In this same general area, a utility building was rolled over and the roof was turn off several storage or utility structures.  Several homes sustained minor roof damage as well.  The entire county was left without power for several hours in the wake of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed quarter-sized hail in the southwest part of the county, about seven miles southwest of Talking Rock or near Jerusalem.  He also estimated that wind gusts were in the 50 to 60 mph range.  Another report from the public indicated that two large pine trees were blown down about one mile west of Tate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck near a young girl on Dodd Boulevard in east Rome causing injury.  A home in the area was also set on fire by a lightning strike.  In addition, a power substation in the northern part of the county was struck by lightning with a resulting fire.  Much of the county was left without power as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that many trees were down from Silver Creek to just southwest of Rome.  A woman was trapped in her home in Rome as a result of a fallen tree on the structure.  She was rescued with no injuries.  The public estimated that wind gusts in the area were near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coweta County 911 Center and the Newnan Times Herald reported that lightning struck six structures within the county.  A home was struck on Mike Powers Road, causing moderate damage to the bedrooms, and Pegasus Trace in Thomas Crossroads, causing just minor damage.  A barn on Roy Road off Georgia Highway 16 in the Raymond community was destroyed as a result of a lightning-related fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong late July cold front and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable atmosphere to produce one of the most widespread outbreaks of severe weather seen across the Peachtree City forecast area since mid-April.  The was marked by a mesoscale convective complex which began early in the day across northeast Alabama and quickly spread southeast through west central and into central Georgia during the afternoon.  Numerous reports of large hail were received and wind damage was widespread in the path of the convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilkinson County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees, at least 15, were down,scattered about the county.  At least two power lines were also down within the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front was approaching the area from the northeast.  Meanwhile, a low pressure trough in the northeast U.S. continued to provide a cold, unstable northwest flow aloft over the area.  This lead to the development of scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  One of these became severe in southeast Georgia.  All of the storms produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County Emergency Management Director reported penny-sized hail in the western part of the county near the Montgomery county line on Georgia Highway 130, just northwest of South Thompson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County Emergency Management Director reported that a thunderstorm downburst blew down 10 to 20 trees northeast of Lyons.  In addition, one home lost about 40 shingles.  One tree was also down in the city of Lyons as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare and rather strong cold front for July was approaching the area from the northwest during the afternoon of the 11th.  A strong short wave in northwest flow aloft, combined with the cold front and a warm, moist, unstable surface air mass resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms across the area during the afternoon hours.  Abundant cloud cover prevented most of these from becoming severe.  However, as the storms moved into the southern portion of the forecast area, isolated storms produced damaging downburst winds.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was also an issue with some of the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County 911 Center reported that a portion of a roof of a tire business was blown off.  Part of the roof landed on a nearby power line causing it to come down.  In addition, 10 to 15 trees were blown down around the city and a couple of other power lines were also down as a result of downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same upper low that brought a number of large hail storms to north and central Georgia on June 11th and 12th remained over the northeast, but was shifting slightly further east.  Isolated thunderstorms, a few severe, continued to develop during the afternoon, but were confined primarily to north Georgia on this day.  Several produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning, but only isolated storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck three structures in the city, including the 911 center.  Damage there was confined mainly to electronic equipment.  Minor damage occurred to the other structures.  In addition to the lightning, pea-sized hail fell for up to five minutes in the city.

This combined with a persistent warm, moist, air mass over the region resulted in yet another afternoon and evening of showers and thunderstorms.  Isolated storms became severe producing damaging downburst winds and marginally severe hail.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was also an issue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haralson County Emergency Management Director reported that a poultry farm between Bremen and the Carroll county line near Temple was destroyed from an apparent thunderstorm downburst or microburst.  A number of trees were down in the area of the poultry farm and were twisted about with sheet metal from the damaged poultry facility.  Damage to the poultry farm alone was valued at $0.75M.  A couple of trees were also down in the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extended period of dry conditions, a widespread thunderstorm event during the afternoon and early evening hours brought welcome rain to the area, but unfortunately a number of severe thunderstorms as well.  The thunderstorms were fueled by a stationary front lying across central Georgia combined with a short wave in northwest flow aloft as a large trough developed across the eastern U.S.  Most of the events were characterized by marginal severe thunderstorm wind and hail events.

The was marked by a mesoscale convective complex which began early in the day across northeast Alabama and quickly spread southeast through west central and into central Georgia during the afternoon.  Numerous reports of large hail were received and wind damage was widespread in the path of the convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County Emergency Management Director reported that about 10 trees were down in the Thomaston area, mainly in the vicnity of Georgia Highway 74 and State Park Road.  One tree was also down in the northern city limits of Thomaston.  Pea-sized hail was observed by an amateur radio operator just within the southern city limits of Thomaston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a home in town on Thurston Avenue.  Minor damage was reported to the clothes dryer, where the lightning strike occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the Georgia/Tennessee line and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable air mass to produce widespread thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  Several of these became severe producing hail and damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator relayed a report of two lightning-related house fires.  The Clayton County Emergency Management Director reported that one of the homes suffered substantial damage when lightning started a fire in the drop ceiling of the basement when it struck a panel box outside of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of weak low pressure aloft persisted across the south central and southeast U.S.  This resulted in a continuation of scattered afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms across the southern states.  Storms on this particular day were all non-severe, although many produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.  Isolated storms produced locally heavy rain and wind gusts up to 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck two homes, one in Peachtree City on Smokey Way and another in Fayetteville on Chateau Drive.  Moderate damage resulted to both structures.  In addition, a person was struck and injured by lightning on Winter Valley Court in Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms produced damaging winds in portions of Quitman and Worth counties in southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down throughout the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early evening thunderstorms produced damaging winds in portions of Quitman and Worth counties in southwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down southeast of Quitman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted and actually worsened during September and October.  October, climatologically the driest month of the year anyway, fell even short of normal values at most locations in north and central Georgia.  Rainfall deficits of 15 to 20 inches were common in the north and rainfall deficits of 6 to 12 inches were common in central areas.     Most areas of the state had only received 30 to 40 percent of normal annual rainfall by the end of October.  Many lakes and rivers across north and central Georgia were nearing all time record low levels and above ground water supplies were being significantly impacted in many of the larger cities, especially Atlanta.  A number of stream gage locations on creeks and river in north and central Georgia had established new record low water levels during October, including the major river basins of the Coosa, Chattahoochee, upper Oconee, upper Ocmulgee and Flint. Significant water conservation measures were being implemented in many cities across north and central Georgia.||Note, the impact of the drought on crops and the estimated monetary damage was included with the September 2007 Storm Data.  Additional crop losses from September to October should be minimal in comparison as October marks the end of the growing season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted and actually worsened during September and October.  October, climatologically the driest month of the year anyway, fell even short of normal values at most locations in north and central Georgia.  Rainfall deficits of 15 to 20 inches were common in the north and rainfall deficits of 6 to 12 inches were common in central areas.     Most areas of the state had only received 30 to 40 percent of normal annual rainfall by the end of October.  Some cities were well on track to having their driest year since records had been kept back into the late 1800s.  Many lakes and rivers across north and central Georgia were nearing all time record low levels and above ground water supplies were being significantly impacted in many of the larger cities, especially Atlanta.  A number of stream gage locations on creeks and rivers in north and central Georgia had established new record low water levels during October, including the major river basins of the Coosa, Chattahoochee, upper Oconee, upper Ocmulgee, and Flint. Significant water conservation measures were being implemented in many cities across north and central Georgia.||Note, the impact of the drought on crops and the estimated monetary damage was included with the September 2007 Storm Data.  Additional crop losses from September to October should be minimal in comparison as October marks the end of the growing season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Atlanta television stations reported that lightning struck and set on fire a home in Morrow.  The event was being covered live via a television helicopter.  The Daily Herald of McDonough reported that lightning struck the steeple of Dixon Grove Baptist Churchon Fielder Street in Jonesboro.  There was a small fire at the base of the steeple where it connected to the roof.  In addition, lightning knocked out power to the Clayton State University Campus causing cancellation of all classes for the remainder of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in a moisture rich environment, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The parking area at the Memorial Medical Center was reported to be under 1 foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in a moisture rich environment, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were reported flooded in Savannah. A few of the streets that were reported to be flooded include, Abercorn and  Derenne, East 67th Street from Abercorn to Derenne, 54th Street and  Reynolds, 56th Street and reynolds, 56th Street and Battey, and 56th Street and Habersham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure in a moisture rich environment, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Riceboro. A wall cloud was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1.5 ft of standing water at an underpass. Additionally, a major road in town was closed due to high water, and it was reported to be impassable by vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was reported that water was starting to rise into housing units in the Belfast Siding area of Richmond Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening High pressure over New England and low pressure off the Southeast U.S. coastline, resulted in a tight pressure gradient across southern South Carolina and Southeast Georgia. Strong Northeast winds and High Astronomical Tides combined to produce significant Beach Erosion across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant Beach Erosion took place along area beaches as High Astronomical Tides combined with strong Northeast winds across the coastal waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were reported impassable in Savannah. A few streets that were reported to be impassable included, Paulsen Street and 63rd Street, Paulsen Street and 61st Street, East Victory and Live Oak, Victory and Abercorn around Ardley Park, the 500 block of Pennsylvania Avenue, Derenne Avenue, Bull Street, 62nd Street and Spring Hill, and Pine Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door cold front was approaching north and east Georgia from the Carolinas.  Meanwhile...aloft a large upper high remained centered just west of the area.  These features combined with hot afternoon temperatures and a moist low-level air mass to result in scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  The activity was somewhat more widespread, more intense, and further north than had been observed on several previous days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County Emergency Management Director reported that at least three homes and one business were struck by lighting resulting in fires and minor to moderate damage to the structures.  A home on New Castle Drive in Fayetteville was a total loss and suffered $275,000 in damages.  Another home on Georgia Highway 314 suffered $100,000 in damages, another on Buckeye Lane $10,000, and minor damage to a business at the Fayette Pavilion Shopping Center about two miles north of Fayetteville.  There were several other lightning related calls to the fire department that evening, but no damage was reported at the other locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette County Emergency Management Director reported that at least two homes and a work shop sustained damage from lightning strikes and related fires.  A home in the Lakemont Subdivision southwest of Fayetteville on Wind Ridge Dr was a total loss with $450,000 in damages.  Another home on Creek View Trail northwest of Fayetteville was also a total loss with $250,000 in damages.  In addition, a workshop on the east side of the county on Morgan Mill Road was destroyed with $75,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong late July cold front and northwest flow aloft combined with a moist and unstable atmosphere to produce one of the most widespread outbreaks of severe weather seen across the Peachtree City forecast area since mid-April. Numerous reports of large hail were received and wind damage was widespread in the path of the convective complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fayette Citizen reported that lightning struck a home in the Wood Creek subdivision just south of the Peachtree City city limits.  Damage to the home was significant.  In addition, three fire fighters sustained minor injuries while fighting the fire.  The Fayette County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning also struck a home on Stone Haven Drive about 5 miles southwest of Fayetteville.  The home was a total loss from the resulting fire.  Another home in the High Grove subdivision near by suffered minor damage from a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into northwest Georgia combined with abundant low-level moisture and instability, provided the impetus for scattered afternoon thunderstorms once again.  While none of these reached severe limits, an lightning incident was reported from Floyd county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a home east of Woodstock.  The home suffered minor damage as a result.  There were also several areas without power because of lightning strikes.  One tree was also down just east of Woodstock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines and a large tree blown down around the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Milltown Rd with large limbs blown down near the intersection of highway 51 and Boleman Hill Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pattern of a broad upper trough in the south central U.S. continued.  This combined with a persistent warm, moist, air mass over the region resulted in yet another afternoon and evening of showers and thunderstorms.  Isolated storms became severe producing damaging downburst winds and marginally severe hail.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was also an issue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett County Emergency Management Director reported that three homes were struck by lightning, one in Lawrenceville on Polarwood Lane Northwest, another on Wildwood Lake Drive Northwest in Suwanee, and a third on Amsterdam Court in Lilburn.  The only one which sustained any significant damage was the one on Wildwood Lane in Suwanee.  The Gwinnett Daily Post reported that lightning struck a tree next to a home on Coteau Place in Lawrenceville.  The tree fell on the roof of the home causing moderate damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Management Director, along with the county 911 center and a storm spotter all reported that trees and power lines were down in both the Wrightsville and Adrian areas.  At least three trees and two power lines were down in Wrightsville and one tree and power line were down in the Adrian community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local television media from Columbus reported that a few trees were down on U.S. Highway 27 in northeast Muscogee county.  One tree was also down on and blocking Warm Springs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft was still the primary weather feature for the area.  However, the center of the high had drifted west of the region, allowing northwest flow aloft to develop over the region. A weak cold front was located across central Georgia.  This provided the focus for scattered afternoon thunderstorms.  A few of these storms became strong to severe, with damaging wind the primary element observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County 911 Center reported that six trees were blown down by strong thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muscogee County 911 Center reported that at least 10 trees were down in the Midland area.  Some of the trees fell on power lines.

Low pressure moved west-northwest across the Florida penisula and into the eastern Gulf of Mexico.  On Wednesday night 9/20-21/2007 a rainband developed over central Florida and moved rapidly northward into north Florida and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 1500 ft damage path extending from the south-southeast to the north-northwest and ending near Lovett Road and Landrum Lane. Twelve structures had slight to moderate damage to roofs and skirting.  Most structures were mobile homes and outbuildings. A few trees and power lines were also blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County 911 Center reported that more than a dozen trees were blown down across the county.  One downed tree caused a power pole to be damaged along Georgia Highway 96 in Warner Robins.  The public reported that a large and heavy piece of outdoor furniture was blown off a deck and numerous large limbs were down from pecan and pine trees.  In Perry, a fire was ignited at the Shady Rest Apartments on Second Street when a tree fell onto a power line.  Two of the apartments were completely destroyed.  Another tree fell down at the neighboring church playground.  A wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at Warner Robins Air Force Base at 342 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual summer cold front continued to move southeast through Georgia.  By afternoon it had moved through the northwest corner of the state.  Northwest flow and cold air aloft continued to provide an unstable air mass across the area.  This combined with daytime heating led to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms once again.  Isolated storms became severe and most produced frequent cloud-to-ground lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Times Georgian of Carrollton reported that a weekend type cabin was destroyed by a fire as a result of a lightning strike.  The cabin was declared a total loss.  The fire was not actually discovered until after a midnight by a driver passing by the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of extreme northeast Georgia during July. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. The absence of rain negatively affected the hay crop, creating concern for the loss of livestock. Dollar values for the drought damage should be included in either the August or September Storm Data for this region.
A few severe storms affected extreme northeast Georgia during the afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare and rather strong cold front for July was approaching the area from the northwest during the afternoon of the 11th.  A strong short wave in northwest flow aloft, combined with the cold front and a warm, moist, unstable surface air mass resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms across the area during the afternoon hours.  Abundant cloud cover prevented most of these from becoming severe.  However, as the storms moved into the southern portion of the forecast area, isolated storms produced damaging downburst winds.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was also an issue with some of the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union Recorder of Milledgeville reported that several trees and power lines were down in the area.  Several trees were down on roads and power lines.  The public reported that thunderstorm winds blew down two large pine trees causing damage to a fence and destroying a new trampoline about six miles west-southwest of Milledgeville.  Approximately 1500 people in the Milledgeville area were without power during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line with a LEWP develped just west of the state and spread east across the CSRA and Midlands producing widespread wind damage and some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees and powerlines down.  One man died when storms swept across the area. 40-year-old Robert Walker was riding his bike when a tree fell, killing him.|Nearby, there were four others trapped in a store on the corner of 11th and Dugas Street. Inmate crews were out for hours today cleaning up the mess.|The tree that leveled the corner store was completely uprooted. Two men and two children were inside the building and were injured when it collapsed.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure at the surface and aloft resulted in excessively hot and humid conditions across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices reached 114 degrees at Savannah International Airport.  Heat indices across much of southeast Georgia topped out between 114 and 118 degrees as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over the northeast U.S. was providing a colder northwest flow aloft to the southeast states.  Meanwhile, a weak front had stalled just south of the area.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of the 25th and continued into the early morning hours of the 26th.  A similar situation was repeated during the afternoon of the 26th, although the focus had shifted a little further south into central Georgia at that time.  The storms produced intense cloud-to-ground lightning, marginal severe hail, and some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail on Hartline Road.

Damage was confined to a portion of an interior wall.

Water covered all four lanes of the road. Around 1 pm local time, at least 10 streets in Baxley had standing water due to heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions persisted across northeast Georgia through September, as the region experienced another month of well-below normal precipitation. By the end of the month, most locations were running a yearly rainfall deficit of 11-17 inches. Stream flows and groundwater levels were near record low levels, with many streams running at 5 percent or less of normal flow. Water levels on area reservoirs were some of the lowest in recorded history. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. Farmers continued to struggle to feed livestock due to a lack of hay and poor pasture conditions, forcing many cattle to be sold or slaughtered. Agricultural and other losses attributed to the drought are estimated to be in the hundreds of millions of dollars. County-based losses for the growing season will be included in next month's Storm Data.

August 2007 proved to be one of the hottest months on record for much of north and central Georgia as a large ridge of high pressure combined with abnormally dry conditions from several preceding months of below normal rainfall.  Numerous daily record high temperatures were broken, especially during the mid part of the month.  Even some record high minimum values were recorded during this period.  It proved to be the warmest month ever (since records have been kept back into the 1800s) for Atlanta and tied the record for the most number of days that 100 was reached or exceeded in one summer for Atlanta.  While most of the month was above normal, the core of the intense heat was during the period from August 7th to August 22nd.  Similar situations were observed at the other reporting stations.||Two heat related deaths were reported, one in Atlanta and the other in Rome.  The Atlanta-Journal and Constitution reported that an 87-year old woman was found dead in her home on the morning of August 12th as a result of the heat.  She was found in a closed apartment with the air conditioning turned off.  Her body temperature was determined to be 102 degrees.  This was the first heat-related death in Georgia since July 2006.  The Rome News Tribune reported that a 65-year old man was found dead in his camper trailer at Roys Trailer Park near Rome on August 13th.  The man was found closed in his hot trailer.  The air conditioning was not functioning properly.||The excessive heat combined with continued below normal rainfall to exacerbate the drought conditions across the region.||Details of the excessive heat for Atlanta, Athens, Columbus, and Macon are provided below:||Atlanta:||Average temperature for the month:  85.6F which was 6.7F degrees above normal.  This goes down not only as the hottest August ever (83.8 in 1980 was previous record), but it was the warmest month on record for Atlanta (85.3 in July of 1993 was old record).||Number of times temperature reached or exceeded 100:  8 ||Hottest day: 104 on 8/22||Record high maximum temperatures were set on the following eight days:  8/9 (103), 8/10 (103), 8/11 (100), 8/13 (100), 8/14 (99),  8/17 (101), 8/22 (104), and 8/23 (98).||The reading of 104 on 8/22 was the all-time record high for the month of August.||Record high minimum temperatures were set on these nine days:  8/5 (75), 8/8 (82), 8/9 (79), 8/10 (78), 8/11 (81), 8/14 (78), 8/16 (79), 8/19 (76), and 8/22 (79).||The reading of 82 on 8/8 was the all-time record high minimum temperature for the city of Atlanta.|______________________________________________________________________||Athens:||Average temperature for the month: 85.2 which was 6.8 degrees above the normal.  It also ties July of 1993 for the warmest month ever at Athens.  The previous record average temperature for the month was 83.4 in 1983.||Number of times temperature reached or exceeded 100: 13||Hottest day: 106 on 8/22||Record high maximum temperatures were set on the following nine days:  8/7 (101), 8/8 (102), 8/9 (105), 8/10 (104), 8/11 (102), 8/15 (103), 8/16 (105), 8/22 (106), and 8/26 (99).||Note:  The record high for August is 107, which was reached on 8/20/1925 and 8/21/1983.||Record high minimum temperatures were set on five days:  8/8 (75), 8/9 (78), 8/11 (79), 8/17 (76), and 8/22 (75).||The reading of 79 on 8/11 ties the monthly and all-time record high minimum temperature for Athens.||______________________________________________________________________||Columbus:||Average temperature for the month:  85.2 which was 3.9 degrees above normal.  This sets a new record for the warmest August ever (old one was 83.4 in 1983) and falls just short of the warmest month ever, which was 85.3 in July of 1993.||Number of times temperature reached or exceeded 100:  6||Hottest day: 104 on 8/10||Record highs were set on the following four days:  8/9 (100), 8/10 (104)*, 8/11 (103), and 8/22 (102).||The temperature of 104 on 8/10 ties the record for the warmest temperature in August set on 8/18/2000.  This also coincides with the warmest temperature of all-time at Columbus.||Record high minimum temperatures were set on the following five days:  8/5 (77), 8/7 (77), 8/11 (81), 8/22 (77), and 8/23 (77).||Note: the record for the warmest low ever in the month is 82 set back on 8/2/1999.|_______________________________________________________________________||Macon:||Average temperature for the month:  84.6 which was 4.6 degrees above normal.  This ties the record for the month, which also occurred in 1954 and 1980. An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Morgan County 911 Center reported that a number of trees were down in the far southeast part of the county, just southwest of Buckhead, blocking Seven Islands Road.  Pea-sized hail was also observed in the area.

Severe to extreme drought conditions persisted across much of extreme northeast Georgia during August. Stream flows and groundwater levels approached record low levels. Water levels on some reservoirs decreased by as much as 1 foot every 10 days. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. Dollar values for the drought damage should be included in either the September or October Storm Data for this region.

A weak cold front was located across central Georgia.  An inverted trough was moving west through the eastern Gulf.  These combined to result in the typical summertime afternoon and evening thunderstorms, a few of which became strong to severe.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was particularly significant with the thunderstorms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official with the Monroe County Emergency Management Agency reported that an apparent thunderstorm downburst or microburst caused considerable damage throughout the city of Forsyth and along Interstate-75 from mile marker 193 in the northwest part of the county to mile marker 185 just southeast of Forsyth.  The damage consisted mainly of downed trees throughout this area, including many throughout the city of Forsyth.  Several homes were damaged as a result of the downed trees and several power lines were down as well.  Johnstonville Road at mile marker 193 was blocked from downed trees and power lines.  Several roads were blocked in two subdivisions in the Forsyth area as a result of downed trees.  The Riatta subdivision appeared to be the hardest hit where in addition to tree, home, and power line damage, some horse stables were damaged by the strong winds.  It was also noted that there were several traffic accidents along Interstate-75 between mile markers 195 and 183.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was located across central Georgia.  An inverted trough was moving west through the eastern Gulf.  These combined to result in the typical summertime afternoon and evening thunderstorms, a few of which became strong to severe.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was particularly significant with the thunderstorms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official with the Monroe County Emergency Management Agency reported localized flooding as a result of very heavy rain from a small thunderstorm that passed from northwest to southeast across the county.  The Forsyth area was particuarly hard hit where drainage systems were unable to handle the amounts of rain that fell in a 30 minute period of time.  Several streets were flooded for a brief period.  One building sustained minor damage when several inches of water got into the bottom floor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees and power lines were down around the city, including Sandy Springs, Dunwoody, the city of Atlanta, and parts of south Fulton county.  The Atlanta Journal and Constitution reported that several thousand people in North Fulton county were left without power immediately following the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was located across central Georgia.  An inverted trough was moving west through the eastern Gulf.  These combined to result in the typical summertime afternoon and evening thunderstorms, a few of which became strong to severe.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was particularly significant with the thunderstorms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barrow County 911 Center reported that two power lines were down as the result of lightning strikes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was located across central Georgia.  An inverted trough was moving west through the eastern Gulf.  These combined to result in the typical summertime afternoon and evening thunderstorms, a few of which became strong to severe.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was particularly significant with the thunderstorms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington County 911 Center reported that lightning started a brush fire along Montgmery Road in the southwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summertime pattern continued across north and central Georgia as high pressure aloft drifted just west of the area.  The storms were very small, but with high temperatures near 100 across the area, the dissipating storms were capable of wind damage.  Such occurred with one small storm in Oconee county which produced a downburst and brought down a number of trees and power lines in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oconee County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a building in the city and set it on fire.  A damage amount was indeterminable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was approaching the area from the northwest as the upper ridge, which had persisted over the area much of the month, retreated back to the west and a weak trough of low pressure developed over the eastern U.S.  As with previous days, thunderstorms developed rapidly during the mid-afternoon and quickly moved south.  However, the activity was in general less numerous and less intense than on previous days.  The main problem this day was cloud-to-ground lightning and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alpharetta Fire & Rescue Department reported that a 15-year old male was killed from a lightning strike.  The individual was riding his bicycle with a group of friends in a parking lot at an apartment complex in the vicinity of 500 Governor's Circle.  The group attempted to seek shelter from the thunderstorm under a gazebo.  However, the victim was unable to seek shelter before the incident occurred.  He was found unconscious in the parking lot next to his bicycle and was pronounced dead at North Fulton Regional Hospital.

However, the activity was in general less numerous and less intense than on previous days.  The main problem this day was cloud-to-ground lightning and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Management Director reported that a tree was blown down onto a home near Avon and Cascade Avenues in southwest Atlanta.  While there were no injuries, the home suffered significant damage.  About six miles to the southeast of this location, a wind gust of 53 mph was measured by the ASOS located at the Atlanta Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport.  Much of the airport and the ASOS are actually located just across the border in northern Clayton county.

However, the activity was in general less numerous and less intense than on previous days.  The main problem this day was cloud-to-ground lightning and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jackson County Sheriff's Department reported that several trees were blown down northeast of Jefferson.  One mobile home was damaged when a tree fell on the home.

This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees and some power lines were blown down in the Bolingbroke area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forsyth County Emergency Management Director reported that two homes were struck and set on fire by lightning.  One of the homes sustained substantial damage.  Damage to the other home was less severe, but still significant.  Lightning also struck a transformer in the area and left about 5000 residents in the area without power for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front was approaching the area from the northwest as the upper ridge, which had persisted over the area much of the month, retreated back to the west and a weak trough of low pressure developed over the eastern U.S.  As with previous days, thunderstorms developed rapidly during the mid-afternoon and quickly moved south.  However, the activity was in general less numerous and less intense than on previous days.  The main problem this day was cloud-to-ground lightning and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Americus Times Recorder reported that lightning struck and killed four horses that were grazing in a pasture off Georgia Highway 377 in the central part of the county, south of Americus.  The horses were huddled under a pecan tree when they were struck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense upper ridge covered most of the southern U.S.  This resulted in maximum temperatures reaching the 100 to 105 range across much of north and central Georgia.  While the atmosphere was very stable, the intense heat combined with low-level moisture resulted in isolated afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  The storms were small and short-lived, but one produced a damaging wind gust in Jones county during the evening.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was also a problem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fox Channel 24 television of Macon reported that a small and brief thunderstorm produced wind gusts causing a couple of trees and power lines to be blown down along the Griswoldville shortcut off Georgia Highway 49.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense upper ridge covered most of the southern U.S.  This resulted in maximum temperatures reaching the 100 to 105 range across much of north and central Georgia.  While the atmosphere was very stable, the intense heat combined with low-level moisture resulted in isolated afternoon and evening thunderstorms.  The storms were small and short-lived, but one produced a damaging wind gust in Jones county during the evening.  Cloud-to-ground lightning was also a problem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbus Ledger-Inquirer reported that lightning struck near a school complex causing damage to the fire alarm systems and air conditioners at three schools, Carver Middle School, Harris County High School, and Mulberry Creek Elementary school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into northwest Georgia combined with abundant low-level moisture and instability, provided the impetus for scattered afternoon thunderstorms once again.  While none of these reached severe limits, an lightning incident was reported from Floyd county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Management Director reported that lightning struck a tree causing it to fall down onto a home.  Minor damage resulted.  In addition, wind gusts from thunderstorms caused three trees to be blown down in Rome.

High pressure aloft was still the primary weather feature for the area.  However, the center of the high had drifted west of the region, allowing northwest flow aloft to develop over the region. A weak cold front was located across central Georgia.  This provided the focus for scattered afternoon thunderstorms.  A few of these storms became strong to severe, with damaging wind the primary element observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County Emergency Management Director reported that a thunderstorm downburst or microburst resulted in considerable damage across the northwest and north central part of the county, but especially in the Vidalia area.  More than 30 large oak trees were blown down in the city of Vidalia, proper, with a scattering of other trees down from northwest of Vidalia to near Santa Claus southeast of Vidalia.  The worst path of damage extended from just northwest of Vidalia, southeastward through the heart of the city, to just south of town.  Several homes were damaged, some from downed trees and others had their roofs blown off.  Furthermore, a Hardees restaurant under construction in Vidalia was destroyed when the wind blew down the frame.  Several roads were blocked from downed trees, including portions of Georgia Highway 130 and U.S. Highway 1.  More than a dozen power lines were also down throughout the northwest part of the county.  Much of the city of Vidalia and surrounding areas were left without power, forcing many residents to take shelter in hotels or Red Cross facilities to escape the heat.  The Automated Weather Observation Station (AWOS) located in Vidalia recorded a 61 mph wind gust around 440 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summertime pattern continued across north and central Georgia as high pressure aloft drifted just west of the area.  The storms were very small, but with high temperatures near 100 across the area, the dissipating storms were capable of wind damage.  Such occurred with one small storm in Oconee county which produced a downburst and brought down a number of trees and power lines in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oconee County Emergency Management Director reported that a thunderstorm downburst or microburst from a small thunderstorm caused considerable damage around the city of Watkinsville.  The roof was blown off two chicken houses.  In addition, two large feeding containers were blown from the chicken houses into an adjacent field.  Twenty to 30 trees were blown down around the city.  Trees were down on power lines at several locations and several roads were blocked from downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The presence of an area of surface low pressure southwest of the area, an approaching frontal boundary from the northwest, and abundant weak upper level disturbances in a deep tropically moist environment, resulted in heavy rainfall and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported on a property located on Omeler Loop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions persisted across northeast Georgia through September, as the region experienced another month of well-below normal precipitation. By the end of the month, most locations were running a yearly rainfall deficit of 11-17 inches. Stream flows and groundwater levels were near record low levels, with many streams running at 5 percent or less of normal flow. Water levels on area reservoirs were some of the lowest in recorded history. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. Farmers continued to struggle to feed livestock due to a lack of hay and poor pasture conditions, forcing many cattle to be sold or slaughtered. Agricultural and other losses attributed to the drought are estimated to be in the hundreds of millions of dollars. County-based losses for the growing season will be included in next month's Storm Data.

A weak cold front was approaching the area from the northwest as the upper ridge, which had persisted over the area much of the month, retreated back to the west and a weak trough of low pressure developed over the eastern U.S.  As with previous days, thunderstorms developed rapidly during the mid-afternoon and quickly moved south.  However, the activity was in general less numerous and less intense than on previous days.  The main problem this day was cloud-to-ground lightning and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alpharetta Fire & Rescue Department reported that lightning struck a home and set it on fire.  Fire officials estimated a 30 percent less of the structure as a result of the fire.

An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that a small thunderstorm with a downburst or microburst blew a portion of a roof off a home on Hawkins Road.  An adjacent shed was completely destroyed.  Two trees were also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cobb County 911 Center reported penny-sized hail in the far eastern portion of the county near the Fulton county line or about five miles east of Sandy Plains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Harris County 911 Center reported that several trees and a few power lines were down along and near Georgia Highway 118, southwest of Pine Mountain.  Some power outages were reported in the area.

An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Webster County Emergency Management Director reported quarter-sized hail around the Centerpointe community.  There were reports that a number of cars in the area sustained damage from the hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Troup County 911 Center reported that lightning struck a home and set it on fire.  Damage estimates to the home were indeterminate.  A couple of trees were also struck by lightning causing them to fall.  An electrical transformer was also struck and rendered inoperative by a lightning strike in the same general area.  Finally, fire fighters responded to a grass fire on Stovall Road that was caused by a lightning strike.

Severe to extreme drought conditions persisted across much of extreme northeast Georgia during August. Stream flows and groundwater levels approached record low levels. Water levels on some reservoirs decreased by as much as 1 foot every 10 days. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. Dollar values for the drought damage should be included in either the September or October Storm Data for this region.

Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that an apparent thunderstorm microburst or downburst resulted in more than six trees being sheared off in the Morrow area.  One tree was completely blown down.  In addition, one home lost a three foot section of shingles off its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although high pressure aloft was reasserting itself over the southeast states, a weak front was draped across north Georgia.  This combined with northwest flow aloft, hot surface temperatures, and a very moist and unstable air mass resulted in yet another day of scattered to numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, several of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs County Sheriff's Office reported that a couple of trees were down, one on Sgoda Road and another on Marion Road in the northwest part of the county.  A power line was also down on Marion Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A typical summertime pattern continued across north and central Georgia as high pressure aloft drifted just west of the area.  The storms were very small, but with high temperatures near 100 across the area, the dissipating storms were capable of wind damage.  Such occurred with one small storm in Oconee county which produced a downburst and brought down a number of trees and power lines in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Times of Gainesville reported that several trees were down in and around the city of Cleveland.  Most of the damage was concentrated in the area around Georgia 75 and Georgia 115.  A couple of homes were damaged by downed trees and one tree fell on a Ford Explorer in a parking lot of a boutique shop.  Power outages were widespread across the city with about 500 people in the county left without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions persisted across much of extreme northeast Georgia during August. Stream flows and groundwater levels approached record low levels. Water levels on some reservoirs decreased by as much as 1 foot every 10 days. Agricultural interests continued to be especially hard hit. Dollar values for the drought damage should be included in either the September or October Storm Data for this region.

An unusual mid-August cold front combined with antecedent very hot temperatures in the 95 to 100 degree range and northwest flow aloft to create a very unstable atmosphere.  Thunderstorms developed during mid-afternoon in northwest and west central Georgia and quickly became severe.  Thunderstorm wind damage was the most common occurrence with these storms, but some hail was reported with some of the storms, especially those near the beginning of the event.  Frequent cloud-to-ground lightning was observed with many of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Management Director reported that several trees were down east of Woodstock, several blocking roads.  One home in the area also sustained damage when two trees fell on the structure.  The Cherokee Tribune reported that several power lines were also down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across North Georgia.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto tracked from southern Louisiana during the morning, across central Mississippi during the day, and into northern Alabama and northwest Georgia by evening.  Meanwhile, a strong fall cold front was moving rapidly southeast from the central plains.  The right quadrant of the remnants of Hurricane Humberto tracked across northwest Georgia bringing heavy rain to that portion of the state.  Rainfall amounts of four to six inches occurred across the far northwest portion of Georgia, especially in Murray, Whitfield, and northwestern Fannin counties, but average amounts in this area were more in the two to three inch range.  Some roads were flooded as a result.  In addition, during the afternoon heating, a few thunderstorms became severe across north Georgia producing marginally severe hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that at least seven large trees, some as large as two to three feet in diameter, were down in the Stockbridge area.  One tree was down across Pinehurst Drive.  Several street signs were also blown down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across North Georgia.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto tracked from southern Louisiana during the morning, across central Mississippi during the day, and into northern Alabama and northwest Georgia by evening.  Meanwhile, a strong fall cold front was moving rapidly southeast from the central plains.  The right quadrant of the remnants of Hurricane Humberto tracked across northwest Georgia bringing heavy rain to that portion of the state.  Rainfall amounts of four to six inches occurred across the far northwest portion of Georgia, especially in Murray, Whitfield, and northwestern Fannin counties, but average amounts in this area were more in the two to three inch range.  Some roads were flooded as a result.  In addition, during the afternoon heating, a few thunderstorms became severe across north Georgia producing marginally severe hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny-sized hail in the Stockbridge area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Humberto was the only hurricane to strike the U.S. coastline during the 2007 hurricane season.  The hurricane, which rapidly strenghted from a tropical storm off the upper Texas coast late on the 12th, moved inland near the Texas-Louisiana border early on the 13th.  The remnants of Hurricane Humberto then tracked across central Louisiana, central Mississippi, and into northern Alabama on the 14th.  The center of the remnant of the track moved across extreme northwest Georgia, Dade and Walker county, on the 14th.  With north Georgia being located within the favorable right quadrant of the track, much needed rainfall fell across north central and northwest parts of the state, mainly northwest of a Rome to Gainesville line.  Rainfall amounts of two to three inches were common within this area, with isolated five to six  inch rainfall amounts in parts of Whitfield and Murray counties.  Further south, rainfall amounts were generally one inch or less.  However, afternoon heating combined with the final feeder band associated with Humberto to bring a line of heavy showers and thunderstorms to north Georgia during the afternoon.  A few trees were blown down and there were a couple of reports of hail.  However, no significant damage was reported within Georgia as a result of the remnants of Humberto.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitfield County 911 Center reported that heavy rain, on the order of three to four inches across the area in a four hour period, had resulted in road closures in the north part of Dalton, specifically Willowdale Road at U.S. Highway 41, and also a little further north along Georgia Highway 71, Cleveland Highway, near Wailing Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical low pressure area was moving west across the northern Gulf of Mexico.  At 7 am EST on the 21st, the tropical low was located just off the Florida coast near Pensacola.  Bands of showers and thunderstorms were rotating counterclockwise around the low into south and as far north as the southern portions of central Georgia.  Some weak rotation was noted in the stronger thunderstorms.  One of these thunderstorms moved north into Sumter county causing wind damage in the southeast part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Emergency Management Director reported that thunderstorm wind gusts blew down several trees and power lines.  In addition, the roof was blown off a porch of one home in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Citizen of Dalton reported that many trees were down across both the northern and southern portions of the county.
